topics.title: Isothermal Batch Reactor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL507 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Isothermal Batch Reactor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Isothermal Batch Reactor (IBR)** is a closed, constant-volume vessel in which a measured quantity of reactants is charged all at once, the contents are held at a strictly constant temperature throughout the reaction, and the system is allowed to react for a specified residence time without any inflow or outflow of mass. Because temperature is held constant by an external heating/cooling medium, the rate constant $k$ does not vary with time, and the reaction progress becomes a pure function of the elapsed reaction time $t$.

In KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory terminology, the isothermal batch reactor experiment is conducted to **experimentally determine the specific reaction rate constant ($k$) and the reaction order ($n$) at a fixed temperature** for a liquid-phase homogeneous reaction, by withdrawing samples at known time intervals and analyzing the unreacted concentration (typically by titration or conductivity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCHL507 – Module 2):**
> The isothermal batch reactor experiment uses the canonical **saponification of ethyl acetate with sodium hydroxide** (second-order, $2^{nd}$ order, $A + B \rightarrow Products$) conducted in a temperature-controlled stirred-tank glass reactor. The student must compute $k$ at two different temperatures, verify the Arrhenius relation, and confirm the integrated rate law for a $2^{nd}$ order reaction with equal initial concentrations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an isothermal batch reactor like a **pressure cooker that is carefully held at exactly one temperature setting** while you watch a chemical reaction slowly proceed inside.

- **Batch** = like a cooking pot. You put all ingredients in *once* at the start, the lid stays on (no material enters or leaves), and you simply wait while the cooking (reaction) happens.
- **Isothermal** = the stove is perfectly regulated. The temperature never drifts up or down, so the reaction speed stays predictable. If the stove fluctuated, the food would cook at changing speeds, and your timing would be ruined.
- The **sample tap** on the side is like dipping a small spoon out at fixed times to test how done the food is. Each tiny sample tells you how far the reaction has progressed.

The most important real-world laboratory analogue is the **saponification reaction** (soap-making from oils), which is why this reaction is chosen — it is safe, inexpensive, fast enough to observe, and easy to analyze by acid-base titration with **HCl (0.1 N)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box — Isothermal Batch Reactor**
> A **transient**, **closed system**, **perfectly mixed** reactor operating at **constant temperature**, **constant volume**, and **zero feed/product flow**, in which composition changes only as a function of **time** $t$ (not space).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** |
| Standard lab temperature | $T$ | **303 K (30 °C) to 333 K (60 °C)** |
| Initial concentration of ethyl acetate | $C_{A0}$ | **0.05 to 0.1 kmol/m³** |
| Initial concentration of NaOH | $C_{B0}$ | **Equal to $C_{A0}$** (equimolar) |
| Sample volume withdrawn | $V_s$ | **5 mL per sample** |
| Titrant | HCl | **0.1 N (standardized)** |
| Indicator | Phenolphthalein | Pink → Colorless end point |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Batch Reactor Mole Balance — The Foundation Equation

For any well-mixed batch reactor, the general **mole balance on component $A$** reduces to a pure ordinary differential equation in time:

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A \cdot V$$

Since volume $V$ is constant (liquid-phase reaction with no significant density change), we can divide by $V$ to convert to a concentration form:

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A$$

This is the **single most important equation in the entire experiment**. It is the starting point from which all integrated rate laws are derived.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in the lab:** Every analytical technique you use (titration, conductivity) measures $C_A$ at a specific time $t$. The differential equation above is what links the measured concentrations to the rate $r_A$.

### 2.2 Rate Laws for Common Reaction Orders

The rate of disappearance of $A$ follows a power-law form:

$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A^n$$

where:
- $k$ = specific reaction rate constant (units depend on $n$)
- $n$ = order of reaction with respect to $A$
- $C_A$ = concentration of $A$ at time $t$

For the **saponification reaction** (the standard KTU experiment), the reaction is **second order overall, first order in each reactant**:

$$A + B \rightarrow Products$$

$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A \cdot C_B$$

When $C_{A0} = C_{B0}$ (equimolar), then $C_A = C_B$ at all times, and the rate law simplifies to a **pseudo-second-order** form:

$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A^2$$

### 2.3 Integrated Rate Laws (Kinetics at Constant T)

Because the reactor is isothermal, $k$ is constant and the ODE can be directly integrated.

**Zero-order reaction:**

$$C_A = C_{A0} - k \cdot t$$

**First-order reaction:**

$$C_A = C_{A0} \cdot e^{-k t} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \ln\!\left(\frac{C_{A0}}{C_A}\right) = k \cdot t$$

**Second-order reaction (with $C_{A0} = C_{B0}$):**

$$\frac{1}{C_A} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = k \cdot t$$

**n-th order reaction:**

$$C_A^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n} = (n-1) \cdot k \cdot t$$

### 2.4 The Arrhenius Relation — Temperature Dependence

When the experiment is repeated at **two different temperatures** ($T_1$ and $T_2$), the rate constants $k_1$ and $k_2$ are related by:

$$k = k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

A **plot of $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$** gives a straight line of slope $-E_a/R$, allowing determination of the **activation energy** $E_a$ (in J/mol or kJ/kmol).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we use Arrhenius in the lab:** The two-temperature experiment is a classic KTU requirement. It teaches the student that reaction rates are not just functions of concentration — they are exponential functions of temperature.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Application / Plot to Verify | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = -k\,C_A^{\,n}$ | Differential form (starting point) | kmol·m⁻³·s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $C_A = C_{A0} - k\,t$ | Zero-order: $C_A$ vs $t$ is linear | kmol·m⁻³ |
| 3 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_{A0}}{C_A}\right) = k\,t$ | First-order: $\ln C_{A0}/C_A$ vs $t$ linear | s⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{C_A} - \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}} = k\,t$ | **Second-order (equimolar): $1/C_A$ vs $t$ linear** | m³·kmol⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $C_A^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n} = (n-1)\,k\,t$ | General $n$-th order integrated form | depends on $n$ |
| 6 | $C_{A,\text{blank}} - C_A = k\,C_{A0}\,C_{B0}\,t$ | Saponification with non-equimolar feeds | kmol·m⁻³ |
| 7 | $k = k_0\,e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius temperature dependence | various |
| 8 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1}-\dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Two-temperature $E_a$ calculation | J·mol⁻¹ |
| 9 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{k\,C_{A0}}$ | Half-life for $2^{nd}$ order equimolar | s |
| 10 | $C_A = \dfrac{(V_{\text{HCl,blank}} - V_{\text{HCl,t}})\,N_{\text{HCl}}}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | Concentration from titration | kmol·m⁻³ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Isothermal batch reactors are the workhorse of:
- **Pharmaceutical batch synthesis** (drug intermediates, small-volume fine chemicals)
- **Polymerization reactors** (emulsion, suspension)
- **Fermentation & bioprocess industries** (vaccine, enzyme production)
- **Specialty chemicals** (perfumes, dyes, adhesives)

The kinetic data obtained from a bench-scale isothermal batch reactor is the **fundamental input** for designing continuous stirred-tank reactors (CSTR) and plug-flow reactors (PFR) at industrial scale — making this experiment a critical bridge between laboratory chemistry and plant design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Second-Order Integrated Rate Law (Equimolar Case)

This is the **central derivation** for the KTU saponification experiment and the one most frequently asked in the university exam.

**Step 1 — Write the differential rate law.**
For a $2^{nd}$ order reaction with $C_{A0} = C_{B0}$:

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k \cdot C_A^2$$

**Step 2 — Separate the variables.**
Move all $C_A$ terms to the left and $t$ terms to the right:

$$-\frac{dC_A}{C_A^{\,2}} = k \cdot dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides between the limits.**
At $t = 0$, $C_A = C_{A0}$. At $t = t$, $C_A = C_A$:

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} -\frac{dC_A}{C_A^{\,2}} = \int_0^t k \cdot dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals.**
The left side gives:

$$\left[\frac{1}{C_A}\right]_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = \frac{1}{C_A} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}}$$

The right side gives:

$$k \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — Combine to obtain the working equation.**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{C_A} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = k \cdot t\;}$$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of measurable titration volume.**
With $C_A \propto (V_{\text{blank}} - V_t)$, define the **conversion** $X_A$ as:

$$C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$$

Substitute:

$$\frac{1}{C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = k\,t$$

$$\frac{X_A}{C_{A0}(1 - X_A)} = k\,t$$

This is the **plot-friendly** form used to extract $k$ graphically from a plot of $\dfrac{X_A}{1 - X_A}$ vs $t$, where the slope equals $k \cdot C_{A0}$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Arrhenius Two-Point Form

**Step 1 — Write the Arrhenius equation at two temperatures.**

$$k_1 = k_0 \, e^{-E_a / R T_1} \quad \text{and} \quad k_2 = k_0 \, e^{-E_a / R T_2}$$

**Step 2 — Take the ratio $k_2 / k_1$ to eliminate the pre-exponential factor $k_0$.**

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\!\left[-\frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Reorder the bracket by factor of $-1$.**

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\;}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the activation energy.**

$$E_a = R \cdot \frac{\ln(k_2 / k_1)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical example:** If $T_1 = 303$ K, $T_2 = 313$ K, $k_1 = 0.005$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹, $k_2 = 0.012$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹, then $E_a \approx 70{,}600$ J/mol — a typical value for saponification.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full Lab-Calculations Style)

**Given:**
- Reaction: $CH_3COOC_2H_5 + NaOH \rightarrow CH_3COONa + C_2H_5OH$
- $C_{A0} = C_{B0} = 0.05$ kmol/m³
- $V_{\text{blank}} = 25.0$ mL of 0.1 N HCl
- $T = 303$ K

| $t$ (s) | $V_t$ (mL) | $C_A$ (kmol/m³) | $1/C_A$ (m³/kmol) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 25.0 | 0.0500 | 20.0 |
| 300 | 19.8 | 0.0396 | 25.25 |
| 600 | 16.4 | 0.0328 | 30.49 |
| 900 | 14.0 | 0.0280 | 35.71 |
| 1200 | 12.2 | 0.0244 | 40.98 |

**Concentration formula used:**

$$C_A = \frac{(V_{\text{blank}} - V_t) \times 0.1}{V_{\text{sample}}} \quad \text{(in mol/L, then convert to kmol/m³)}$$

**Linear-regression result (slope = $k$):**
The straight-line fit of $1/C_A$ vs. $t$ gives:

$$\text{slope} = k = 0.0175 \text{ m}^3 \cdot \text{kmol}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Interpretation:** $k$ at $303$ K $\approx 1.75 \times 10^{-2}$ m³·kmol⁻¹·s⁻¹, equivalent to $1.75 \times 10^{-2}$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹ — the correct order of magnitude for saponification in aqueous medium.

### 3.4 Conversion & Half-Life Expressions

| Order | $X_A(t)$ Expression | Half-life $t_{1/2}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $X_A = \dfrac{k\,t}{C_{A0}}$ | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{C_{A0}}{2k}$ |
| 1 | $X_A = 1 - e^{-k\,t}$ | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ |
| 2 (equimolar) | $X_A = \dfrac{k\,C_{A0}\,t}{1 + k\,C_{A0}\,t}$ | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{1}{k\,C_{A0}}$ |

For 2nd-order reactions, **doubling the initial concentration halves the half-life**, which is a quick experimental check on whether the reaction is genuinely second-order.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Isothermal Batch Reactor Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    reactantA[Reactant A Ethyl Acetate] --> mixTank[Stirred Glass Reactor 2 L Capacity]
    reactantB[Reactant B Sodium Hydroxide] --> mixTank
    thermoBath[Thermostatic Water Bath Constant Temperature] --> mixTank
    tempSensor[Digital Temperature Sensor PT-100] --> thermoBath
    controller[PID Temperature Controller] --> thermoBath
    stirrer[Mechanical Stirrer 300 RPM] --> mixTank
    mixTank --> sampleTap[Sample Withdrawal Port 5 mL]
    sampleTap --> quench[Quench in Cold Water]
    quench --> titration[Titrate Against 0.1 N HCl]
    titration --> dataLog[Concentration vs Time Data]
    dataLog --> plotA[Plot 1 over Ca vs t]
    plotA --> slopeA[Slope equals k at Temperature T1]
    dataLog --> plotB[Plot 1 over Ca vs t at T2]
    plotB --> slopeB[Slope equals k at Temperature T2]
    slopeA --> arrhenius[ln k vs 1 over T plot]
    slopeB --> arrhenius
    arrhenius --> Ea[Activation Energy Ea in kJ per kmol]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram of the Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1 Clean and Dry Reactor] --> B[Step 2 Charge 1 L Ethyl Acetate Solution]
    B --> C[Step 3 Pre-heat to Set Temperature T1]
    C --> D[Step 4 Add 1 L Pre-heated NaOH Solution Start Stop Watch]
    D --> E[Step 5 Withdraw 5 mL Sample Every 3 Minutes]
    E --> F[Step 6 Quench and Titrate with HCl]
    F --> G[Step 7 Record Vt vs t Data]
    G --> H[Step 8 Compute Ca and 1 over Ca]
    H --> I[Step 9 Linear Regression to Find k1 at T1]
    I --> J[Step 10 Repeat at T2 and T3]
    J --> K[Step 11 Arrhenius Plot ln k vs 1 over T]
    K --> L[Step 12 Report k and Ea]
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic for Determining Reaction Order from Lab Data

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawData[Time vs Concentration Data] --> testZero[Test Zero Order: Ca vs t Linear]
    testZero -->|Yes| reportZero[Report n equals 0]
    testZero -->|No| testFirst[Test First Order: ln Ca0 over Ca vs t Linear]
    testFirst -->|Yes| reportFirst[Report n equals 1]
    testFirst -->|No| testSecond[Test Second Order: 1 over Ca vs t Linear]
    testSecond -->|Yes| reportSecond[Report n equals 2 Saponification Case]
    testSecond -->|No| testN[Test n-th Order Fit]
    testN --> reportN[Report Empirical n]
    reportZero --> calcK[Calculate k from Slope]
    reportFirst --> calcK
    reportSecond --> calcK
    reportN --> calcK
    calcK --> done[Report k and Order with Units]
```

### 4.4 Schematic of Reactor Assembly (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph reactorVessel[Glass Reactor Vessel 2 L Three Neck Round Bottom Flask]
        motorDrive[Stirrer Motor Drive Shaft]
        thermowell[Thermowell with PT-100 Sensor]
        samplingPort[Glass Sampling Port with Rubber Septum]
    end
    subgraph utilities[Utility Systems]
        waterBath[Constant Temperature Water Bath Julabo Type]
        circulator[Water Circulator Pump]
        coolingCoil[Internal Cooling Coil Optional]
    end
    subgraph analysis[Analysis Station]
        burette[Digital Burette 0.1 N HCl]
        beaker[250 mL Conical Flask with Phenolphthalein]
        balance[Analytical Balance for Reagent Weighing]
    end
    reactorVessel --> utilities
    samplingPort --> analysis
    waterBath --> circulator
    circulator --> reactorVessel
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows represent the physical direction of fluid/material flow, while the dashed conceptual links show how the temperature control loop closes through the water bath back into the reactor jacket — this is the heart of the "isothermal" condition.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define an isothermal batch reactor. Why is it called "transient" and "closed"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An isothermal batch reactor is a closed vessel in which all reactants are charged initially, the contents are perfectly mixed, and the temperature is held constant by an external medium. It is called **closed** because no material enters or leaves during the reaction (no inlet, no outlet streams), and **transient** because composition, concentration, and conversion all vary with elapsed reaction time $t$, never reaching a steady state. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the integrated rate law for a second-order reaction with equimolar initial concentrations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For a second-order reaction $-r_A = k C_A^2$ with $C_{A0} = C_{B0}$:

$$\frac{1}{C_A} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = k \cdot t$$

This means a plot of $1/C_A$ versus $t$ is a straight line passing through the origin with slope $k$, having units of **m³·kmol⁻¹·s⁻¹** (or L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹ in CGS). (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Module Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 2]**
*(a)* Derive the integrated rate law for a second-order reaction of the form $A + B \rightarrow Products$ with $C_{A0} = C_{B0}$. Show the linearized plotting form. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* In a saponification experiment at 35 °C, the following data were obtained. The initial concentration of both ethyl acetate and NaOH is 0.05 kmol/m³. Determine the second-order rate constant $k$ at 35 °C. **(7 marks)**

| $t$ (s) | $V_t$ (mL of 0.1 N HCl) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 25.0 |
| 240 | 19.0 |
| 480 | 15.5 |
| 720 | 13.1 |
| 960 | 11.4 |

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Step 1: Write the differential rate law:

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k \cdot C_A^2$$

Step 2: Separate variables:

$$-\frac{dC_A}{C_A^{\,2}} = k \cdot dt$$

Step 3: Integrate from $t=0$ to $t=t$:

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} -C_A^{-2} \, dC_A = \int_0^t k \, dt$$

Step 4: Evaluate:

$$\left[\frac{1}{C_A}\right]_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = k \cdot t$$

Step 5: Final integrated form:

$$\frac{1}{C_A} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = k \cdot t \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Linearized plotting form (2 marks):**
Equation (i) is in $y = mx$ form where $y = 1/C_A$, $x = t$, slope = $k$, and $y$-intercept = $1/C_{A0}$. A plot of $1/C_A$ vs $t$ yields a straight line; $k$ is obtained from the slope.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Convert titration volumes to concentrations using $C_A = (V_{\text{blank}} - V_t) \times N_{\text{HCl}} / V_{\text{sample}}$. With $V_{\text{blank}} = 25.0$ mL and $V_{\text{sample}} = 5$ mL:

| $t$ (s) | $V_{\text{blank}} - V_t$ (mL) | $C_A$ (kmol/m³) | $1/C_A$ (m³/kmol) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0 | 0.0000 | ∞ (start) |
| 240 | 6.0 | 0.0120 | 83.33 |
| 480 | 9.5 | 0.0190 | 52.63 |
| 720 | 11.9 | 0.0238 | 42.02 |
| 960 | 13.6 | 0.0272 | 36.76 |

*(Note: $C_A$ above is the **consumed** part. For plotting, the **remaining** concentration is $C_{A,\text{remaining}} = C_{A0} - C_{A,\text{consumed}}$. Adjust accordingly in lab notebook.)*

**Step 2:** Apply least-squares fit to $1/C_{A,\text{remaining}}$ vs $t$:

**Slope $k \approx 0.0186$ m³·kmol⁻¹·s⁻¹** (≈ 0.0186 L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹).

**Step 3:** State the final answer with units:
$k_{35°C} = 0.0186$ m³·kmol⁻¹·s⁻¹

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — KTU Module Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*(a)* Explain the principle, procedure, and analysis of the saponification of ethyl acetate in a batch reactor. Mention the role of the thermostatic water bath. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* The second-order rate constant for saponification was found to be $0.012$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹ at 30 °C and $0.024$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹ at 40 °C. Calculate the activation energy $E_a$ using the Arrhenius equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Principle (3 marks):** Saponification of ethyl acetate by NaOH is an irreversible, homogeneous, second-order liquid-phase reaction:

$$CH_3COOC_2H_5 + NaOH \rightarrow CH_3COONa + C_2H_5OH$$

The reaction is conducted in an isothermal batch reactor; samples are withdrawn at known time intervals, and the unreacted NaOH is determined by titration with standard HCl. From the concentration-time data, the second-order rate constant $k$ is computed using $1/C_A - 1/C_{A0} = k t$.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Charge 1 L of ethyl acetate solution (pre-heated) into the reactor.
2. Maintain the reactor at the set temperature $T$ using a thermostatic water bath.
3. Add 1 L of pre-heated NaOH solution (equimolar), start the stopwatch.
4. Withdraw 5 mL samples at fixed intervals (e.g., every 3 min).
5. Quench each sample in cold distilled water and titrate with 0.1 N HCl using phenolphthalein.
6. Record $V_t$ vs $t$ and compute $C_A$ and $1/C_A$.

**Role of thermostatic water bath (2 marks):** The bath continuously circulates temperature-controlled water around the reactor jacket, ensuring that the reaction mixture temperature remains constant (within ±0.5 °C). This is what makes the reactor **isothermal**, so that $k$ is genuinely a constant and the integrated rate laws (with constant $k$) are valid.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $T_1 = 303$ K, $k_1 = 0.012$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹; $T_2 = 313$ K, $k_2 = 0.024$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹; $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.

**Step 1:** Apply the two-point Arrhenius equation:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the left-hand side:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{0.024}{0.012} = 2.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln(2.0) = 0.6931$$

**Step 3:** Compute the right-hand side bracket:

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{303} - \frac{1}{313} = \frac{313 - 303}{303 \times 313} = \frac{10}{94{,}839} = 1.0544 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $E_a$:

$$E_a = \frac{0.6931 \times 8.314}{1.0544 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a = \frac{5.7625}{1.0544 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a \approx 54{,}652 \text{ J/mol} \approx 54.65 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Final answer:** $E_a \approx 54.65$ kJ/mol — a value consistent with the literature for saponification in aqueous medium.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in the Isothermal Batch Reactor Question:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $T$ to Kelvin** before plugging into the Arrhenius equation. This single error wipes out 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Swapping $T_1$ and $T_2$** in the bracket $(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$; a sign error flips the sign of $E_a$ and is treated as a major error.
> 3. **Using $C_{A,\text{consumed}}$ instead of $C_{A,\text{remaining}}$** when computing $1/C_A$ — the plot becomes non-linear and the student incorrectly concludes the reaction is not second order.
> 4. **Reporting $k$ without units** — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for missing units, since the unit itself is evidence that the order has been correctly identified.
> 5. **Forgetting the blank titration** — the $V_{\text{blank}}$ reading is essential; without it, $C_A$ cannot be calculated.
> 6. **Insufficient sample size** — withdrawing < 5 mL makes the HCl titration end-point ambiguous.
> 7. **Not maintaining the temperature** during sampling — the open sample port lets heat in/out; close the port quickly to maintain isothermal condition.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Isothermal Batch Reactor**
>
> - **Closed, perfectly mixed, constant-volume, constant-temperature** vessel. No inflow, no outflow.
> - The governing differential equation is $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = -k\,C_A^{\,n}$.
> - The general integrated rate law is $C_A^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n} = (n-1)\,k\,t$ for $n \neq 1$.
> - **2nd-order equimolar (saponification):** $\dfrac{1}{C_A} - \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}} = k\,t$ → plot $1/C_A$ vs $t$ for slope = $k$.
> - **1st-order:** $\ln(C_{A0}/C_A) = k\,t$ → plot $\ln C_{A0}/C_A$ vs $t$ for slope = $k$.
> - **Half-life** is the time to reach $C_A = C_{A0}/2$; for 2nd order, $t_{1/2} = 1/(k\,C_{A0})$.
> - **Arrhenius:** $k = k_0\,e^{-E_a/RT}$; two-point form: $\ln(k_2/k_1) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$.
> - **Units of $k$** depend on order: s⁻¹ (1st), m³·kmol⁻¹·s⁻¹ (2nd), m³·kmol⁻¹·(s·...)⁻¹ (general).
> - The **titration formula** is $C_A = (V_{\text{blank}} - V_t)\,N_{\text{HCl}} / V_{\text{sample}}$.
> - The **thermostatic bath** is the heart of the isothermal assumption — never bypass it.
> - The **reaction order** is determined *experimentally* by trial plots; do not assume it from the stoichiometry.
> - Always report $k$ in **consistent SI units** and convert all $T$ to **Kelvin** before using Arrhenius.
> - The saponification of ethyl acetate is the **canonical KTU experiment** — it is 2nd order overall, 1st order in each reactant.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isothermal Plug Flow Reactor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL507 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Isothermal Plug Flow Reactor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Isothermal Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** is an ideal tubular reactor model in which the reaction mixture flows as a coherent "plug" with no axial mixing (no back-mixing) while complete radial mixing is assumed. The temperature is held constant throughout the reactor length, making the system purely concentration-dependent, which simplifies the rate equations to functions of composition alone.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PCCHL507 – Chemical Reaction Engineering Lab**, the Isothermal PFR experiment is designed to verify the integral design equation of a tubular reactor using a model reaction system (commonly the **saponification of ethyl acetate with sodium hydroxide**, or the **inversion of sucrose** in academic setups) and to determine the **rate constant (k)** of the reaction under ambient, isothermal conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A PFR is a reactor where fluid elements move through the reactor in a single, well-defined direction (piston-like), with no element overtaking or mixing with its neighbors, but with uniform composition in the radial direction at any given axial position.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long, narrow train of carriages moving down a single railway track. Each carriage carries a group of passengers, and **no passenger is allowed to step out and into another carriage while the train is in motion**. Every carriage enters the tunnel at time $t_0$ and exits at time $t_0 + \tau$, where $\tau$ is the **residence time**. Because the carriages do not interact, the passengers in the first carriage (which entered earliest) have spent the most time in the tunnel, while those in the last carriage have spent almost no time. This is the essence of Plug Flow.

**Key intuition points:**
- A long, thin, horizontal tube through which fluid is pumped at a steady rate.
- Each "slice" of fluid behaves like a batch reactor that travels from inlet to outlet, but no slice talks to its neighbor.
- **Temperature is held constant** → no energy balance needed → only mass balance matters.
- The concentration of reactants therefore decreases **monotonically along the length** of the tube.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Reminder:** Reactor performance is benchmarked against **Space Time ($\tau$)**, defined as the time required to process **one reactor volume ($V$)** of feed at inlet conditions: $\tau = V/\nu_0$. For KTU labs, the volumetric flow rate is typically measured using a **rotameter** with a **calibration curve** supplied in the lab manual.

## 1.3 The Saponification Reaction (Standard KTU Lab Reaction)

The most commonly used model reaction in KTU-affiliated CRE labs is the alkaline hydrolysis of ethyl acetate:

$$ \text{CH}_3\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COONa} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} $$

This is a **second-order, elementary, liquid-phase reaction** with rate law:

$$ -r_A = k \cdot C_A \cdot C_B $$

Where:
- $C_A$ = Concentration of ethyl acetate (mol/L)
- $C_B$ = Concentration of NaOH (mol/L)
- $k$ = Second-order rate constant (L/(mol·min))
- $r_A$ = Rate of disappearance of A (mol/(L·min))

Because stoichiometry dictates that $C_A$ and $C_B$ decrease at the **same molar rate** (1:1 stoichiometry), and if the inlet concentrations are **equimolar** ($C_{A0} = C_{B0}$), then $C_A = C_B$ throughout the reactor, simplifying the rate law to:

$$ -r_A = k \cdot C_A^2 $$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Assumptions of the Ideal PFR

The derivation of the PFR design equation rests on **six non-negotiable assumptions**, which examiners frequently test:

1. **Steady-state operation:** $\partial C / \partial t = 0$ at every point.
2. **Isothermal conditions:** $T(x) = T_0 = \text{constant}$ — this is the defining feature of *this* experiment.
3. **No axial mixing (pure plug flow):** Each fluid element is a closed system traveling at the same velocity.
4. **Perfect radial mixing:** Composition is uniform across any cross-section perpendicular to flow.
5. **Constant fluid density:** Volumetric flow rate $\nu$ is constant along the reactor (valid for liquid-phase, dilute, isothermal systems).
6. **Single, well-defined reaction** with known stoichiometry and rate law.

## 2.2 The Design Equation: Step-by-Step Logic

Consider a differential cylindrical element of reactor volume $dV$ at axial position $x$.

- **Molar flow in:** $F_A$
- **Molar flow out:** $F_A + dF_A$
- **Rate of disappearance of A by reaction** within $dV$: $(-r_A)\,dV$

At steady state, the mass balance on species $A$ gives:

$$ F_A = (F_A + dF_A) + (-r_A)\,dV $$

$$ \Rightarrow dF_A = -(-r_A)\,dV = r_A\,dV $$

Since $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ for a single reaction, we have $dF_A = -F_{A0}\,dX_A$. Substituting:

$$ -F_{A0}\,dX_A = r_A\,dV $$

Rearranging and integrating from inlet (0) to outlet (V):

$$ \int_0^V \frac{dV}{F_{A0}} = \int_0^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A} $$

This yields the **fundamental PFR design equation**:

$$ \boxed{\frac{V}{F_{A0}} = \int_0^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A} } \quad \text{or} \quad \boxed{\tau = C_{A0} \int_0^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}} $$

## 2.3 Why Isothermal Simplification Matters

Without isothermal assumption, both the material and energy balances must be solved **simultaneously**, since $-r_A$ becomes a function of both $C_A$ **and** $T$. By holding $T$ constant, the energy balance decouples, and the rate constant $k$ is a known constant throughout the reactor.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\tau = V/\nu_0$ | Space time (residence time) | min or s |
| 2 | $F_{A0} = \nu_0 \cdot C_{A0}$ | Molar feed rate of A | mol/min |
| 3 | $V = F_{A0} \int_0^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | General PFR design equation | L |
| 4 | $\tau = C_{A0} \int_0^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | Space-time form of PFR equation | min |
| 5 | $k = \frac{1}{\tau}\left(\frac{1}{C_{Af}} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}}\right)$ | 2nd-order PFR with $C_{A0}=C_{B0}$ | L/(mol·min) |
| 6 | $k\tau = (1-\varepsilon)\ln(1-X_A) - \varepsilon X_A / (1+\varepsilon X_A)$ | Density change correction (rare in lab) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $X_A = 1 - \dfrac{C_{Af}}{C_{A0}}$ | Definition of fractional conversion | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_{A0} = \dfrac{M_{NaOH}}{M.W._{NaOH} \cdot V_{soln}}$ | Initial molar concentration | mol/L |
| 9 | $V_{reactor} = \pi r^2 L$ | Geometric volume of tubular PFR | cm³ or mL |
| 10 | $C_t = \dfrac{V_{\text{HCl titrant}}}{V_{\text{sample}}} \times C_{HCl}$ | Titration formula for residual NaOH | mol/L |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pipe-Symbol Rule:** All absolute value notations in the table (e.g., $\vert C_A \vert$) must be typed using `\vert` in LaTeX to avoid corrupting the markdown table grid.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Isothermal PFR Concept

- **Industrial ethylene polymerization** reactors (high recycle ratio makes them approach PFR behavior).
- **Plug flow tubular reactors** in petrochemical cracking units.
- **Long, narrow pipeline reactors** for chlorination, sulfonation, and nitration.
- **Pharmaceutical flow chemistry** — used for reproducible, scalable synthesis of APIs.
- **Effluent treatment** — long plug-flow digesters in wastewater engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of PFR Design Equation (Full, Exhaustive)

Starting from the general mass balance on a tubular reactor slice:

$$ \text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation} $$

For species $A$ over a differential element $dV$:

$$ F_A - (F_A + dF_A) + r_A\,dV = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}\,dV $$

**Step 1:** At steady state, $\partial C_A / \partial t = 0$:

$$ -dF_A + r_A\,dV = 0 $$

$$ \Rightarrow dF_A = r_A\,dV $$

**Step 2:** Since $r_A$ is the rate of *generation* (negative for disappearance), we conventionally write:

$$ dF_A = -(-r_A)\,dV $$

**Step 3:** Express $F_A$ in terms of conversion. For any single reaction:

$$ F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A) \quad \Rightarrow \quad dF_A = -F_{A0}\,dX_A $$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the differential form:

$$ -F_{A0}\,dX_A = -(-r_A)\,dV $$

$$ \Rightarrow F_{A0}\,dX_A = (-r_A)\,dV $$

**Step 5:** Separate variables and integrate:

$$ \int_0^V dV = F_{A0} \int_0^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A} $$

$$ \boxed{\frac{V}{F_{A0}} = \int_0^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}} $$

This is the **integral form** of the PFR design equation.

## 3.2 Derivation for the Specific Case: Second-Order with Equimolar Feed

For the saponification reaction with $C_{A0} = C_{B0}$ and stoichiometry 1:1:

$$ -r_A = k\,C_A\,C_B = k\,C_A^2 $$

Since $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$:

$$ -r_A = k\,C_{A0}^2 (1 - X_A)^2 $$

Substituting into the design equation:

$$ \tau = \frac{V}{\nu_0} = C_{A0} \int_0^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_A}{k\,C_{A0}^2 (1 - X_A)^2} $$

$$ \tau = \frac{1}{k\,C_{A0}} \int_0^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_A}{(1 - X_A)^2} $$

The integral evaluates to:

$$ \int_0^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_A}{(1 - X_A)^2} = \left[ \frac{1}{1 - X_A} \right]_0^{X_{Af}} = \frac{1}{1 - X_{Af}} - 1 = \frac{X_{Af}}{1 - X_{Af}} $$

Therefore:

$$ \tau = \frac{1}{k\,C_{A0}} \cdot \frac{X_{Af}}{1 - X_{Af}} = \frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{1}{C_{Af}} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}}\right) $$

Where $C_{Af} = C_{A0}(1 - X_{Af})$.

Rearranging to solve for the **rate constant $k$**:

$$ \boxed{k = \frac{1}{\tau}\left(\frac{1}{C_{Af}} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}}\right)} \quad \text{with units of L/(mol·min)} $$

## 3.3 The Linearized Verification Plot (KTU High-Yield Analytical Trick)

A very common **valuation question** in KTU ESE asks the student to verify the second-order nature of the reaction by plotting the appropriate function and showing that it yields a straight line through the origin.

Starting from the design equation for 2nd-order with equimolar feed:

$$ \tau = \frac{1}{k\,C_{A0}} \cdot \frac{X_{Af}}{1 - X_{Af}} $$

Cross-multiplying:

$$ k\,\tau\,C_{A0} = \frac{X_{Af}}{1 - X_{Af}} $$

Since $C_{Af} = C_{A0}(1 - X_{Af})$, we can also write:

$$ \frac{1}{C_{Af}} = \frac{1}{C_{A0}} + k\,\tau $$

**This is the working equation used in the lab.** If we plot $\left(\dfrac{1}{C_{Af}} - \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}}\right)$ on the **Y-axis** against $\tau$ on the **X-axis**, we obtain a straight line passing through the origin with slope equal to $k$.

$$ \underbrace{\left(\frac{1}{C_{Af}} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}}\right)}_{Y} = \underbrace{k}_{\text{slope}} \cdot \underbrace{\tau}_{X} $$

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Lab Data)

**Given:**
- Reactor volume: $V = 250$ mL
- Volumetric flow rate: $\nu_0 = 25$ mL/min
- Inlet NaOH concentration: $C_{A0} = 0.05$ mol/L
- Outlet NaOH concentration (titration): $C_{Af} = 0.0347$ mol/L

**Step 1: Compute space time**

$$ \tau = \frac{V}{\nu_0} = \frac{250 \text{ mL}}{25 \text{ mL/min}} = 10 \text{ min} $$

**Step 2: Compute conversion**

$$ X_A = 1 - \frac{C_{Af}}{C_{A0}} = 1 - \frac{0.0347}{0.05} = 0.306 $$

**Step 3: Compute rate constant**

$$ k = \frac{1}{\tau}\left(\frac{1}{C_{Af}} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}}\right) $$

$$ k = \frac{1}{10}\left(\frac{1}{0.0347} - \frac{1}{0.05}\right) $$

$$ k = 0.1 \times (28.818 - 20.000) = 0.1 \times 8.818 $$

$$ \boxed{k = 0.8818 \text{ L/(mol·min)} } $$

**Step 4: Repeat for 3–4 different flow rates, plot $\left(\frac{1}{C_{Af}} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}}\right)$ vs $\tau$ and report the slope as $k$.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Isothermal PFR Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Tank - NaOH and Ethyl Acetate Mixture] --> B[Peristaltic Pump or Constant Head Device]
    B --> C[Rotameter - Flow Measurement]
    C --> D[Reactor Inlet Sampling Port]
    D --> E[Isothermal PFR Coil in Constant Temperature Bath]
    E --> F[Reactor Outlet Sampling Port]
    F --> G[Collection Vessel]
    D --> H[Conical Flask - 1 - Inlet Sample for HCl Titration]
    F --> I[Conical Flask - 2 - Outlet Sample for HCl Titration]
    H --> J[Burette with Standard HCl]
    I --> J
    J --> K[End Point - Phenolphthalein Indicator]
    K --> L[Compute CAf and CA0]
    L --> M[Compute tau = V by nu0]
    M --> N[Plot 1 by CAf minus 1 by CA0 versus tau]
    N --> O[Slope equals Rate Constant k]
```

## 4.2 Logic Flow for Calculating Rate Constant from Lab Data

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start of Lab Procedure]) --> S1[Prepare equimolar feed solution of NaOH and Ethyl Acetate]
    S1 --> S2[Measure CA0 by titration against standard HCl]
    S2 --> S3[Set rotameter to desired flow rate and let reactor reach steady state]
    S3 --> S4[Collect outlet sample and titrate against HCl to get CAf]
    S4 --> S5{Is CAf less than CA0?}
    S5 -->|No| S6[Check for leaks and re-stabilize]
    S5 -->|Yes| S7[Compute tau = V divided by nu0]
    S7 --> S8[Compute bracket term 1 by CAf minus 1 by CA0]
    S8 --> S9[Compute k for this run]
    S9 --> S10{More flow rates to test?}
    S10 -->|Yes| S3
    S10 -->|No| S11[Plot Y versus tau and find slope]
    S11 --> S12([Report k and reaction order verification])
```

## 4.3 Schematic Architecture of a Tubular PFR with Sampling Points

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inlet_Zone [Reactor Inlet Section]
        I1[Inlet Reservoir] --> I2[Feed Line with Control Valve]
        I2 --> I3[Inlet T-junction with Sampling Port 1]
    end
    subgraph Reaction_Zone [Isothermal Tubular Reactor in Thermostat]
        I3 --> R1[Coiled Glass Tube Length L1]
        R1 --> R2[Coiled Glass Tube Length L2]
        R2 --> R3[Coiled Glass Tube Length L3]
        R3 --> R4[Coiled Glass Tube Length L4]
    end
    subgraph Outlet_Zone [Reactor Outlet Section]
        R4 --> O1[Outlet T-junction with Sampling Port 2]
        O1 --> O2[Product Collection Beaker]
    end
    I3 -.Titrate.-> T1[Find CA0]
    O1 -.Titrate.-> T2[Find CAf]
    T1 --> Calc[Compute k]
    T2 --> Calc
```

## 4.4 PFR vs CSTR: Side-by-Side Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PFR_Branch [Plug Flow Reactor]
        P1[Concentration drops continuously along length] --> P2[Long residence time distribution tail]
        P2 --> P3[Higher conversion per unit volume than CSTR for positive order]
    end
    subgraph CSTR_Branch [Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor]
        C1[Concentration drops immediately to outlet value] --> C2[Narrow residence time distribution]
        C2 --> C3[Lower conversion per unit volume for positive order]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the assumptions made in deriving the design equation of an ideal isothermal Plug Flow Reactor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The design equation of an ideal isothermal PFR is derived under the following assumptions:
1. Steady-state operation so that $\partial C_A/\partial t = 0$ at every point in the reactor. (1 mark)
2. Isothermal conditions so that the rate constant $k$ is constant along the reactor length. (1 mark)
3. No axial mixing of fluid elements (pure plug flow) and uniform radial composition. (1 mark)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Space Time and Space Velocity. How are they related?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Space Time ($\tau$):** It is the time required to process one reactor volume of feed measured at inlet conditions. $\tau = V/\nu_0$. (1 mark)
- **Space Velocity ($S$):** It is the number of reactor volumes of feed that can be processed per unit time at inlet conditions. $S = \nu_0/V$. (1 mark)
- **Relation:** Space time and space velocity are reciprocals of each other: $S = 1/\tau$. (1 mark)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** Many students incorrectly write the design equation for a PFR and a CSTR in the same form. The PFR equation is an **integral**, while the CSTR equation is a simple algebraic expression. Mixing them up is the **single largest cause of mark deduction** in this topic. Also, students often forget to convert flow rates to consistent units (mL/min vs L/min), which leads to a 2–3 mark penalty.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**a) Derive the integral design equation of an ideal isothermal PFR starting from a differential mass balance on a reacting element of volume $dV$.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Consider a differential volume $dV$ of the reactor at axial position $x$ as shown in the derivation in **Section 3.1**. The general mass balance on species A is:

$$ \text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation} $$

Substituting values:

$$ F_A - (F_A + dF_A) + r_A\,dV = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}\,dV $$

At steady state, $\partial C_A / \partial t = 0$:

$$ -dF_A + r_A\,dV = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad dF_A = r_A\,dV \tag{1} $$

Since $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$, we have $dF_A = -F_{A0}\,dX_A$. Substituting:

$$ -F_{A0}\,dX_A = r_A\,dV $$

Rearranging:

$$ F_{A0}\,dX_A = -r_A\,dV \tag{2} $$

Separating variables and integrating from inlet to outlet:

$$ F_{A0}\int_0^{X_{Af}} dX_A = \int_0^V (-r_A)\,dV $$

$$ F_{A0}\,X_{Af} = \int_0^V (-r_A)\,dV $$

The standard integral form is:

$$ \boxed{\frac{V}{F_{A0}} = \int_0^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}} $$

(7 marks: 1 for the general balance, 2 for steady-state simplification, 2 for substitution of $F_A$ in terms of $X_A$, 1 for separation, 1 for final boxed result.)

**b) For the saponification of ethyl acetate with NaOH carried out in an isothermal PFR, the following data were obtained at 303 K:**

| Run | $\nu_0$ (mL/min) | $C_{Af}$ (mol/L) | $C_{A0}$ (mol/L) |
|-----|------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 20 | 0.0391 | 0.05 |
| 2 | 30 | 0.0421 | 0.05 |
| 3 | 50 | 0.0451 | 0.05 |
| 4 | 70 | 0.0466 | 0.05 |

Reactor volume $V = 200$ mL. **Verify the second-order nature of the reaction and determine the rate constant $k$.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

For a 2nd-order reaction with equimolar feed:

$$ \frac{1}{C_{Af}} = \frac{1}{C_{A0}} + k\,\tau $$

So plotting $\left(\dfrac{1}{C_{Af}} - \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}}\right)$ vs $\tau$ should give a straight line through the origin with slope $k$.

**Step 1: Compute $\tau$ for each run** ($\tau = V/\nu_0$)

| Run | $\tau$ (min) | $1/C_{Af}$ (L/mol) | $1/C_{Af} - 1/C_{A0}$ (L/mol) |
|-----|--------------|---------------------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | 10.000 | 25.575 | 5.575 |
| 2 | 6.667 | 23.752 | 3.752 |
| 3 | 4.000 | 22.173 | 2.173 |
| 4 | 2.857 | 21.459 | 1.459 |

**Step 2: Linear regression on $(X, Y) = (\tau, 1/C_{Af} - 1/C_{A0})$**

Using the least-squares formula, slope $k$:

$$ k = \frac{N\sum(\tau Y) - \sum\tau \sum Y}{N\sum\tau^2 - (\sum\tau)^2} $$

Plugging in the values (or simply using a graphical plot):

- $N = 4$
- $\sum \tau = 23.524$
- $\sum Y = 12.959$
- $\sum \tau^2 = 169.612$
- $\sum (\tau Y) = 96.501$

$$ k = \frac{4 \times 96.501 - 23.524 \times 12.959}{4 \times 169.612 - (23.524)^2} $$

$$ k = \frac{386.004 - 304.876}{678.448 - 553.378} $$

$$ k = \frac{81.128}{125.070} = 0.6486 \text{ L/(mol·min)} $$

$$ \boxed{k \approx 0.649 \text{ L/(mol·min)} } $$

(7 marks: 2 for the linearized equation, 2 for tabulation, 2 for the slope calculation, 1 for the final value with correct units.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**a) Explain the experimental procedure to determine the rate constant of a second-order liquid-phase reaction in an isothermal PFR. List the apparatus and chemicals required.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus Required (2 marks):**
1. Isothermal Plug Flow Reactor (coiled glass tube, typically 200–500 mL capacity) immersed in a constant-temperature water bath.
2. Feed tank with stirrer.
3. Peristaltic pump or constant-head device for steady flow.
4. Rotameter for flow rate measurement.
5. Conical flasks, pipettes, and a burette for titration.
6. Stopwatch.

**Chemicals Required (1 mark):**
1. Standard Sodium Hydroxide solution (e.g., 0.05 N).
2. Ethyl acetate (AR grade).
3. Standard Hydrochloric acid (HCl) solution for titration.
4. Phenolphthalein indicator.

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Prepare a known volume of equimolar feed solution by mixing NaOH and ethyl acetate in a clean feed tank. (0.5)
2. Determine $C_{A0}$ by titrating an inlet sample against standard HCl using phenolphthalein. (0.5)
3. Set the rotameter to the desired flow rate and start the pump. Allow the reactor to run for at least 3 residence times to reach steady state. (1.0)
4. Collect an outlet sample and titrate it against standard HCl to obtain $C_{Af}$. (0.5)
5. Repeat steps 3–4 for at least 4 different flow rates. (0.5)
6. Compute $\tau = V/\nu_0$ for each run. (0.5)
7. Plot $\left(\dfrac{1}{C_{Af}} - \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}}\right)$ vs $\tau$ and determine the slope as $k$. (0.5)

**b) Compare the performance of an isothermal PFR with a CSTR carrying out the same second-order reaction. Under what conditions do they give the same conversion?** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

For a 2nd-order reaction with equimolar feed:
- PFR: $\tau_{PFR} = \dfrac{1}{k\,C_{A0}} \cdot \dfrac{X_A}{1 - X_A}$
- CSTR: $\tau_{CSTR} = \dfrac{1}{k\,C_{A0}} \cdot \dfrac{X_A}{(1 - X_A)^2}$

Therefore:

$$ \frac{\tau_{CSTR}}{\tau_{PFR}} = \frac{1}{1 - X_A} $$

**Comparison (5 marks):**
- For any positive conversion $X_A > 0$, we have $1 - X_A < 1$, so $\tau_{CSTR}/\tau_{PFR} > 1$, meaning **CSTR always requires a larger volume than PFR** for the same conversion in a 2nd-order reaction. (2)
- The PFR is more efficient because reactants are exposed to the highest concentration near the inlet where the rate is highest. (1.5)
- The CSTR, by contrast, instantly dilutes the feed to the outlet concentration, lowering the driving force for reaction. (1.5)

**Same conversion condition (2 marks):**
- For zero conversion ($X_A = 0$), both reactors give the same $\tau = 0$. (1)
- This is the only mathematical equality; physically, for any meaningful operation, **the PFR is always smaller than the CSTR for the same second-order conversion.** (1)

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PFR design equation (general):** $V/F_{A0} = \int_0^{X_{Af}} dX_A / (-r_A)$
- **Space time formula:** $\tau = V/\nu_0 = C_{A0} \int_0^{X_{Af}} dX_A / (-r_A)$
- **For 2nd-order with equimolar feed:** $1/C_{Af} = 1/C_{A0} + k\tau$
- **Plot:** $Y = 1/C_{Af} - 1/C_{A0}$ vs $X = \tau$ gives slope $k$.
- **Units of $k$ (2nd order):** L/(mol·min) or m³/(kmol·s).
- **Steady-state check:** Always allow 3–5 residence times before sampling.
- **PFR vs CSTR:** PFR is always more efficient for positive-order reactions; ratio is $1/(1-X_A)$ for 2nd order.
- **Isothermal assumption validity:** Only valid when heat of reaction is small, concentrations are low, or reactor is jacketed with precise temperature control.
- **Common lab reaction:** Saponification of ethyl acetate with NaOH.
- **Titration endpoint:** Phenolphthalein turning from pink to colorless marks the HCl endpoint.
- **Typical $k$ value at 303 K for saponification:** ~0.6–0.9 L/(mol·min).
- **Density change correction:** Use $\varepsilon$ only if $\nu$ varies significantly — usually ignored in KTU labs.
- **No axial mixing** is the defining physical assumption of a PFR.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isothermal CSTR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL507 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Isothermal CSTR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Isothermal Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)**, also known as a **Mixed Flow Reactor (MFR)** or **Backmix Reactor (BMR)**, is an idealised flow reactor in which the contents are perfectly and instantaneously mixed such that the composition, temperature, and concentration of the reaction mixture are **uniform throughout the entire reactor volume** and **identical to those in the exit stream**.

Under the **isothermal assumption**, the operating temperature is held constant (typically by an external heat exchanger, thermostatted jacket, or by virtue of the reaction being mildly exothermic/endothermic with negligible heat effects). This decouples the energy balance from the material balance, leaving a single governing equation — the **mole balance on a reactant species A**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim-grade):** "A CSTR is an ideal reactor modelled on the assumption of perfect mixing, where the reaction rate, concentration, and temperature at any point inside the reactor are spatially uniform and equal to the conditions of the effluent stream."

The mole balance over a steady-state isothermal CSTR carrying out a single irreversible reaction of the form:

$$A \rightarrow Products$$

yields the canonical **Design Equation of a Single CSTR**:

$$\frac{V}{F_{A0}} = \frac{\tau}{C_{A0}} = \frac{X}{-r_{A, \text{exit}}}$$

where:
- $V$ = reactor volume in $\mathbf{m^{3}}$
- $F_{A0}$ = molar feed rate of A in $\mathbf{kmol \cdot s^{-1}}$
- $\tau$ = **space time** in seconds
- $C_{A0}$ = inlet concentration of A in $\mathbf{kmol \cdot m^{-3}}$
- $X$ = fractional conversion of A
- $-r_{A}$ = rate of consumption of A evaluated at exit conditions in $\mathbf{kmol \cdot m^{-3} \cdot s^{-1}}$

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Spinning Cup of Coffee":**
> Imagine a perfectly stirred cup of black coffee into which you continuously pour milk from the top while the mixture continuously overflows from a spout on the side. No matter how vigorously or slowly you pour, the colour (concentration) of the liquid **anywhere in the cup — and at the spout — is always identical**. The moment a drop of milk enters, it is instantaneously distributed.
>
> The **CSTR behaves identically**: the moment fresh reactant enters, it is "teleported" (mathematically) to every point in the tank, and the effluent leaving has the **lowest concentration (highest conversion)** achievable in an ideal reactor of that volume for a given flow.

The **isothermal** condition is analogous to the coffee being held at a constant temperature by a heating pad beneath the cup — the heat generated or absorbed by mixing is negligible, so temperature doesn't change.

**Why is this concept pivotal?**
In the KTU 2024 Scheme, virtually every reactor comparison, design problem, and viva question pivots around the CSTR design equation. Mastering it is the gateway to:
- PFR vs CSTR comparisons
- Series and parallel reactor networks
- Multiple-reactor staging
- Autocatalytic reaction design

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Physical Quantities (highlighted in bold)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical SI Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V$ | Reactor volume | $\mathbf{m^{3}}$ | Physical capacity of the tank |
| $v_{0}$ | Volumetric feed rate | $\mathbf{m^{3}/s}$ | Volumetric flow entering the reactor |
| $F_{A0}$ | Molar feed rate | $\mathbf{kmol/s}$ | Moles of A fed per second |
| $C_{A0}$ | Inlet concentration | $\mathbf{kmol/m^{3}}$ | Concentration of A in the feed |
| $C_{A}$ | Exit concentration | $\mathbf{kmol/m^{3}}$ | Concentration of A in the effluent |
| $X$ | Conversion | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Fraction of A converted to products |
| $\tau$ | Space time | $\mathbf{s}$ | Time required to process one reactor volume of feed |
| $SV$ | Space velocity | $\mathbf{s^{-1}}$ | Inverse of space time; throughput indicator |
| $-r_{A}$ | Reaction rate | $\mathbf{kmol/(m^{3} \cdot s)}$ | Rate of disappearance of A |
| $k$ | Rate constant | units depend on order | Temperature-dependent kinetic parameter |

---

### 1.4 Salient Features of an Isothermal CSTR (for viva & theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Perfect mixing** → spatial uniformity of $C_{A}$, $T$, $-r_{A}$
> - **Steady state** → no accumulation, $\frac{dC_{A}}{dt}=0$
> - **Exit conditions = reactor interior conditions** → rate evaluated at $C_{A,\text{exit}}$
> - **Isothermal** → $T$ constant → $k$ constant → energy balance is trivial
> - Operates as a **single ODE-free algebraic equation** — the simplest reactor to model

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1: System Definition**
A feed stream containing reactant A at concentration $C_{A0}$ and volumetric flow rate $v_{0}$ enters a well-mixed vessel of volume $V$. Inside the vessel, the irreversible reaction $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$ occurs at a rate $-r_{A}$. The effluent leaves with concentration $C_{A}$ and the same volumetric flow rate $v_{0}$ (assuming constant density).

**Step 2: The Mole Balance (heart of the derivation)**
Under steady state, the **accumulation term vanishes**. The general mole balance reduces to:

$$\text{IN} - \text{OUT} + \text{GENERATION} = 0$$

$$F_{A0} - F_{A} + r_{A} \cdot V = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$V = \frac{F_{A0} - F_{A}}{-r_{A}}$$

**Step 3: Introducing Conversion**
By definition of fractional conversion $X$:

$$F_{A} = F_{A0}(1 - X)$$

Substituting back:

$$V = \frac{F_{A0} \cdot X}{-r_{A}}$$

**Step 4: The Two Canonical Forms**

$$\boxed{\frac{V}{F_{A0}} = \frac{X}{-r_{A}}} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{\tau = \frac{V}{v_{0}} = \frac{C_{A0} \cdot X}{-r_{A}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the rate evaluated at EXIT conditions?**
> Because of perfect mixing, the **entire reactor volume is exposed to the lowest concentration** the reactant will ever experience — the exit concentration $C_{A}$. The integral of $-r_{A}$ over $V$ therefore reduces trivially to a single multiplication by $V$.

**Step 5: Why "Why" and "How" matter for KTU**
- *Why does a CSTR need a larger volume than a PFR for the same conversion?* Because the rate is evaluated at the *lowest* $C_{A}$ throughout the reactor, whereas a PFR integrates the rate as concentration decays from $C_{A0}$ down to $C_{A}$.
- *How does the CSTR achieve steady state experimentally?* The operator must hold $v_{0}$, $C_{A0}$, and $T$ constant long enough (typically $\geq 3\tau$) for transients to wash out.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the equations used in 90% of KTU CSTR problems.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Unit Check / Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Space time | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{v_{0}}$ | seconds (s) — basic time-of-residence |
| 2 | Space velocity | $SV = \dfrac{1}{\tau} = \dfrac{v_{0}}{V}$ | $\mathbf{s^{-1}}$ — throughput indicator |
| 3 | Conversion definition | $X = \dfrac{F_{A0} - F_{A}}{F_{A0}} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{A}}{C_{A0}}$ | dimensionless, 0 ≤ X ≤ 1 |
| 4 | CSTR design eqn. (general) | $\dfrac{V}{F_{A0}} = \dfrac{X}{-r_{A}}$ | applied at exit conditions |
| 5 | CSTR design eqn. (concentration form) | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0}(C_{A0} - C_{A})}{-r_{A}}$ | for $n^{\text{th}}$-order kinetics |
| 6 | 1st-order CSTR | $X = \dfrac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau}$ | when $-r_{A} = kC_{A}$ |
| 7 | 2nd-order CSTR | $\tau = \dfrac{X}{k C_{A0}(1-X)^{2}}$ | when $-r_{A} = kC_{A}^{2}$ |
| 8 | Zero-order CSTR | $X = \dfrac{k\tau}{C_{A0}}$ | when $-r_{A} = k$ |
| 9 | Levenspiel plot relation | $\dfrac{1}{-r_{A}} = \dfrac{\tau}{C_{A0}}$ | graphical; slope of line yields $\tau$ |
| 10 | Damköhler number (1st order) | $Da = k\tau$ | dimensionless group for 1st-order isothermal CSTR |

---

### 2.3 Worked Connection to Rate Laws

For a general $n^{\text{th}}$-order rate law:

$$-r_{A} = kC_{A}^{n} = kC_{A0}^{n}(1-X)^{n}$$

Substituting into the design equation:

$$\tau = \frac{C_{A0} X}{k C_{A0}^{n}(1-X)^{n}} = \frac{1}{k C_{A0}^{n-1}} \cdot \frac{X}{(1-X)^{n}}$$

This **single equation** yields the $X$–$\tau$ relationship for any reaction order $n$ in a CSTR.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where CSTRs are deployed in industry:**
> 1. **Polymerisation reactors** — bulk and solution polymerisation
> 2. **Fermentation bioreactors** — pharmaceutical, brewing
> 3. **Wastewater treatment** — activated sludge process
> 4. **Liquid-phase nitration, sulphonation, neutralisation**
> 5. **Emulsion polymerisation** for paints, adhesives

The CSTR is favoured where **uniform temperature**, **continuous operation**, and **ease of product withdrawal** matter more than minimum reactor volume.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the CSTR Design Equation

**Starting Point — General Mole Balance Equation (M.B.E.) for a reactor with flow:**

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation}$$

In symbolic form for species A in a control volume $V$:

$$\frac{dN_{A}}{dt} = F_{A0} - F_{A} + \int_{0}^{V} r_{A} \, dV$$

**Step 1: Apply the perfect-mixing assumption**

Because concentration is spatially uniform inside the CSTR, the volumetric integral collapses:

$$\int_{0}^{V} r_{A} \, dV = r_{A} \cdot V$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dN_{A}}{dt} = F_{A0} - F_{A} + r_{A} V$$

**Step 2: Apply the steady-state assumption**

At steady state, accumulation is zero:

$$\frac{dN_{A}}{dt} = 0$$

Thus:

$$0 = F_{A0} - F_{A} + r_{A} V$$

**Step 3: Solve for $V$**

$$V = \frac{F_{A} - F_{A0}}{r_{A}} = \frac{F_{A0} - F_{A}}{-r_{A}}$$

**Step 4: Introduce fractional conversion $X$**

$$F_{A} = F_{A0}(1 - X) \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{A0} - F_{A} = F_{A0} X$$

Substituting:

$$V = \frac{F_{A0} X}{-r_{A}}$$

Dividing both sides by $F_{A0}$:

$$\frac{V}{F_{A0}} = \frac{X}{-r_{A}}$$

**Step 5: Use $F_{A0} = v_{0} C_{A0}$ and $\tau = V / v_{0}$ to get the concentration form:**

$$\frac{V}{v_{0} C_{A0}} = \frac{X}{-r_{A}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\tau}{C_{A0}} = \frac{X}{-r_{A}}$$

Multiplying both sides by $C_{A0}$:

$$\tau = \frac{C_{A0} X}{-r_{A}}$$

This is the **canonical CSTR design equation** used in every KTU numerical.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Closed-Form Conversion Expressions for Common Orders

For an isothermal CSTR with reaction rate law $-r_{A} = k C_{A0}^{n-1}(1-X)^{n-1}$ (a unified form), the design equation becomes:

$$\tau = \frac{1}{k C_{A0}^{n-1}} \cdot \frac{X}{(1-X)^{n}}$$

**Order-by-Order Closed-Form Solutions:**

| Reaction Order $n$ | Rate Law $-r_{A}$ | CSTR Design Equation | Closed-Form $X(\tau)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $k$ | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X}{k}$ | $X = \dfrac{k\tau}{C_{A0}}$ (valid for $X \leq 1$) |
| 1 | $kC_{A}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X}{k C_{A0}(1-X)} = \dfrac{X}{k(1-X)}$ | $X = \dfrac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau}$ |
| 2 | $kC_{A}^{2}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{X}{k C_{A0}(1-X)^{2}}$ | Quadratic in $(1-X)$: solve $\;k C_{A0} \tau (1-X)^{2} - X = 0\;$ |
| $n$ (general) | $kC_{A}^{n}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{X}{k C_{A0}^{n-1}(1-X)^{n}}$ | Implicit; solved numerically |

---

### 3.3 Detailed Numerical Illustration (KTU-style)

**Problem (Worked):**
Aqueous A decomposes by a first-order reaction with $k = 0.12 \; \text{min}^{-1}$. An isothermal CSTR of volume $V = 80 \; \text{L}$ processes a feed with $C_{A0} = 2.5 \; \text{mol/L}$ at a volumetric flow rate $v_{0} = 10 \; \text{L/min}$. Determine (a) space time, (b) exit concentration, (c) fractional conversion.

**Step 1: Compute space time**

$$\tau = \frac{V}{v_{0}} = \frac{80 \; \text{L}}{10 \; \text{L/min}} = 8 \; \text{min}$$

**Step 2: Compute Damköhler number**

$$Da = k\tau = (0.12)(8) = 0.96$$

**Step 3: Apply 1st-order closed-form**

$$X = \frac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau} = \frac{0.96}{1 + 0.96} = \frac{0.96}{1.96} = 0.4898$$

**Step 4: Compute exit concentration**

$$C_{A} = C_{A0}(1 - X) = 2.5 \times (1 - 0.4898) = 2.5 \times 0.5102 = 1.276 \; \text{mol/L}$$

**Step 5: Verify via design equation**

$$\tau = \frac{C_{A0} X}{k C_{A}} = \frac{(2.5)(0.4898)}{(0.12)(1.276)} = \frac{1.2245}{0.15312} = 8.0 \; \text{min} \;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Levenspiel Plot for CSTR

The **Levenspiel plot** is a graphical method universally used in KTU labs to extract kinetic parameters.

**Starting point:**

$$\frac{V}{F_{A0}} = \frac{X}{-r_{A}}$$

For a fixed reactor volume and feed rate, $V/F_{A0}$ is **constant**. Hence, for varying $X$:

$$-\frac{1}{r_{A}} = \frac{V}{F_{A0}} \cdot \frac{1}{X}$$

A plot of $\frac{1}{-r_{A}}$ (ordinate) versus $\frac{1}{X}$ (abscissa) is a **straight line through the origin with slope $V/F_{A0}$**. From this slope, the rate constant can be determined once $C_{A0}$ and the reaction order are known.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab note:** In a typical CSTR experiment, you vary $v_{0}$ (and hence $\tau$), measure $C_{A}$ at each flow rate, compute $X = 1 - C_{A}/C_{A0}$, compute $-r_{A} = k C_{A}^{n}$ from assumed kinetics, and plot $1/(-r_{A})$ vs $1/X$ to validate the model.

---

### 3.5 Comparison Derivation: CSTR vs PFR Volume for Same Conversion

For a 1st-order reaction, PFR volume (per unit feed rate) is:

$$\frac{V_{\text{PFR}}}{F_{A0}} = -\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_{A}} \frac{dC_{A}}{r_{A}} = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{C_{A0}}{C_{A}}\right) = -\frac{1}{k}\ln(1-X)$$

For the CSTR:

$$\frac{V_{\text{CSTR}}}{F_{A0}} = \frac{X}{kC_{A0}(1-X)} = \frac{X}{kC_{A}(1-X)/(1-X)\cdot(1)} = \frac{X}{kC_{A0}(1-X)}$$

The ratio (always $\geq 1$ for 1st order):

$$\frac{V_{\text{CSTR}}}{V_{\text{PFR}}} = \frac{X/(1-X)}{-\ln(1-X)}$$

This ratio is the **classic KTU board proof** that the CSTR is always larger (or equal) than a PFR for positive-order kinetics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Isothermal CSTR Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeFeed["FEED TANK\nC_A0, v0, T0"] --> nodePump["METERING PUMP\nv0 setting"]
    nodePump --> nodeInletValve["INLET VALVE\nFlow control"]
    nodeInletValve --> nodeCSTR["ISOTHERMAL CSTR\nVolume V\nPerfect mixing\nT = constant\nC_A uniform"]
    nodeCSTR --> nodeSample["EXIT SAMPLING PORT\nC_A measurement"]
    nodeCSTR --> nodeOutletValve["OUTLET VALVE"]
    nodeOutletValve --> nodeProduct["PRODUCT TANK\nEffluent collection"]
    nodeHeater["THERMOSTAT JACKET\nHeat input/output\nMaintains isothermal T"] -.-> nodeCSTR
    nodeTempSensor["TEMPERATURE SENSOR\nT readout"] -.-> nodeCSTR
    nodeTempSensor --> nodeController["PID CONTROLLER\nSends signal"]
    nodeController -.-> nodeHeater
    nodeStirrer["IMPELLER STIRRER\nHigh RPM\nEnsures perfect mixing"] -.-> nodeCSTR
```

---

### 4.2 Material Balance Schematic on a CSTR Control Volume

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph controlVol["CONTROL VOLUME = REACTOR"]
        nodeIn["INLET\nF_A0 = v0 * C_A0"]
        nodeVol["REACTOR V V\nPerfect mixing\nSteady state\nIsothermal"]
        nodeGen["GENERATION TERM\nr_A * V\n(Negative for reactant)"]
        nodeOut["OUTLET\nF_A = v0 * C_A\nwhere C_A = C_A uniform inside"]
    end
    nodeIn -->|"Feed stream"| nodeVol
    nodeVol -->|"Effluent stream"| nodeOut
    nodeGen -->|"A is consumed"| nodeVol
```

---

### 4.3 Experimental Procedure Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStep1["STEP 1: Prepare feed of known C_A0"] --> nodeStep2["STEP 2: Set thermostatic jacket to desired T"]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3["STEP 3: Prime reactor and confirm mixing"]
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4["STEP 4: Set volumetric flow v0"]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5["STEP 5: Wait for steady state\nt greater than 3 tau"]
    nodeStep5 --> nodeStep6["STEP 6: Withdraw exit sample\nAnalyse for C_A"]
    nodeStep6 --> nodeStep7["STEP 7: Compute X = 1 minus C_A over C_A0"]
    nodeStep7 --> nodeStep8["STEP 8: Compute minus r_A at exit"]
    nodeStep8 --> nodeStep9["STEP 9: Repeat for 5 to 7 different v0 values"]
    nodeStep9 --> nodeStep10["STEP 10: Plot 1 over minus r_A vs 1 over X\nVerify straight line and extract k"]
```

---

### 4.4 Conversion vs Space Time Curves — Order Effect (Mermaid Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph convTime["FRACTIONAL CONVERSION X vs SPACE TIME tau"]
        nodeZero["ZERO ORDER\nX = k tau over C_A0\nLinear early; saturates at X = 1"]
        nodeFirst["FIRST ORDER\nX = k tau over 1 + k tau\nApproaches 1 asymptotically"]
        nodeSecond["SECOND ORDER\nNon-linear slow start\nSlow approach to X = 1"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** All three curves approach $X \to 1$ for very large $\tau$, but the rate of approach depends strongly on the reaction order. The 1st-order curve is the most commonly encountered in CSTR lab experiments.

---

### 4.5 Multi-CSTR Cascade (preview of advanced topic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF["FEED F_A0, C_A0, v0"] --> nodeR1["CSTR 1\nV1, tau1\nC_A1 = C_A exit"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2["CSTR 2\nV2, tau2\nC_A2 = C_A exit"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3["CSTR N\nVN, tauN\nC_A final"]
    nodeR3 --> nodeP["PRODUCT"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the design equation of a single isothermal CSTR carrying out a reaction $A \rightarrow$ Products, and explain why the rate is evaluated at the exit concentration.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The mole balance on a steady-state isothermal CSTR for species A yields:

$$\frac{V}{F_{A0}} = \frac{X}{-r_{A}}$$

Equivalently, $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} X}{-r_{A}}$.

The rate $-r_{A}$ is evaluated at the **exit concentration** (1 mark) because, under the perfect-mixing assumption, the concentration of A is **spatially uniform throughout the entire reactor volume and is identical to the concentration in the effluent stream** (1 mark). Hence, the entire reactor experiences the lowest concentration that the reactant attains, and the rate is constant everywhere in the tank (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define space time and space velocity for a CSTR. What is the relationship between the two?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Space time** $\tau$ is the time required to process **one reactor volume of feed** at the prevailing feed conditions. $\tau = V / v_{0}$, units of $\mathbf{seconds}$ (1 mark).
- **Space velocity** $SV$ is the **inverse** of space time: $SV = 1/\tau = v_{0}/V$, units of $\mathbf{s^{-1}}$. It represents the number of reactor volumes of feed processed per unit time (1 mark).
- **Relationship:** $SV \cdot \tau = 1$ (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Aqueous A decomposes in an isothermal CSTR of volume $V = 100 \; \text{L}$ following a **first-order rate law** with rate constant $k = 0.25 \; \text{min}^{-1}$. The inlet concentration is $C_{A0} = 3.0 \; \text{mol/L}$. The desired exit conversion is **75%**.

**(a)** [7 marks] Determine the required volumetric flow rate $v_{0}$ of the feed and the corresponding **space time** and **space velocity**.

**(b)** [7 marks] If the flow rate is **doubled**, what is the new exit conversion? Comment on the change in reactor productivity.

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a): [7 marks]**

For a 1st-order CSTR:

$$X = \frac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau}$$

With $X = 0.75$ and $k = 0.25 \; \text{min}^{-1}$:

$$0.75 = \frac{0.25 \tau}{1 + 0.25 \tau}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$0.75 + 0.1875 \tau = 0.25 \tau$$

$$0.75 = 0.0625 \tau$$

$$\tau = 12 \; \text{min}$$

**Space time = 12 min** (1 mark).

**Volumetric flow rate:**

$$v_{0} = \frac{V}{\tau} = \frac{100 \; \text{L}}{12 \; \text{min}} = 8.333 \; \text{L/min}$$

**$v_{0} = 8.333 \; \text{L/min}$** (1 mark).

**Space velocity:**

$$SV = \frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{1}{12} = 0.0833 \; \text{min}^{-1}$$

**$SV = 0.0833 \; \text{min}^{-1}$** (1 mark).

**Verification by direct design equation:**

$$\tau = \frac{C_{A0} X}{k C_{A0}(1-X)} = \frac{X}{k(1-X)} = \frac{0.75}{0.25 \times 0.25} = \frac{0.75}{0.0625} = 12 \; \text{min} \;\checkmark$$

(2 marks for correct approach and complete calculation. 1 mark for units and consistency.)

**Part (b): [7 marks]**

When $v_{0}$ is doubled: $v_{0}' = 16.667 \; \text{L/min}$.

New space time:

$$\tau' = \frac{V}{v_{0}'} = \frac{100}{16.667} = 6 \; \text{min}$$

**New conversion:**

$$X' = \frac{k \tau'}{1 + k\tau'} = \frac{0.25 \times 6}{1 + 0.25 \times 6} = \frac{1.5}{1 + 1.5} = \frac{1.5}{2.5} = 0.60$$

**$X' = 60\%$** (3 marks).

**Productivity analysis:**

Original rate of A consumed: $F_{A0} X = v_{0} C_{A0} X = 8.333 \times 3.0 \times 0.75 = 18.75 \; \text{mol/min}$.

New rate: $v_{0}' C_{A0} X' = 16.667 \times 3.0 \times 0.60 = 30.0 \; \text{mol/min}$.

**Productivity increases** (from 18.75 to 30.0 mol/min) (2 marks), because although conversion falls from 75% to 60% (i.e., a 20% relative drop), the throughput doubles. The reactor is now operating closer to its kinetic limit, and the **net production rate is higher** (1 mark). This illustrates the **trade-off between conversion and throughput** in reactor design (1 mark).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

The decomposition of A in a CSTR follows a **second-order rate law** $-r_{A} = k C_{A}^{2}$ with $k = 0.08 \; \text{L/(mol \cdot min)}$. The reactor volume is $V = 50 \; \text{L}$, and the feed concentration and flow rate are $C_{A0} = 1.5 \; \text{mol/L}$ and $v_{0} = 5 \; \text{L/min}$ respectively.

**(a)** [7 marks] Compute the **exit concentration** $C_{A}$ and the **fractional conversion** $X$.

**(b)** [7 marks] Determine the rate $-r_{A,\text{exit}}$ and the rate at which A is being consumed in $\text{mol/min}$ inside the reactor.

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a): [7 marks]**

**Step 1:** Compute space time.

$$\tau = \frac{V}{v_{0}} = \frac{50}{5} = 10 \; \text{min}$$

**Step 2:** Write the 2nd-order CSTR design equation.

$$\tau = \frac{C_{A0} X}{k C_{A0}^{2}(1-X)^{2}} = \frac{X}{k C_{A0}(1-X)^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to a quadratic in $X$ (or in $1-X$).

$$k C_{A0} \tau (1-X)^{2} = X$$

Substituting $k C_{A0} \tau = 0.08 \times 1.5 \times 10 = 1.2$:

$$1.2(1-X)^{2} = X$$

$$1.2(1 - 2X + X^{2}) = X$$

$$1.2 - 2.4 X + 1.2 X^{2} - X = 0$$

$$1.2 X^{2} - 3.4 X + 1.2 = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve the quadratic.

$$X = \frac{3.4 \pm \sqrt{3.4^{2} - 4 \times 1.2 \times 1.2}}{2 \times 1.2} = \frac{3.4 \pm \sqrt{11.56 - 5.76}}{2.4} = \frac{3.4 \pm \sqrt{5.80}}{2.4}$$

$$X = \frac{3.4 \pm 2.408}{2.4}$$

Two roots: $X = \frac{5.808}{2.4} = 2.42$ (rejected, > 1) and $X = \frac{0.992}{2.4} = 0.4133$.

**Acceptable: $X = 0.4133$** (4 marks).

**Step 5:** Compute exit concentration.

$$C_{A} = C_{A0}(1 - X) = 1.5 \times (1 - 0.4133) = 1.5 \times 0.5867 = 0.880 \; \text{mol/L}$$

**$C_{A} = 0.880 \; \text{mol/L}$** (2 marks). **$X = 41.33\%$** (1 mark).

**Part (b): [7 marks]**

**Step 1:** Compute exit rate.

$$-r_{A} = k C_{A}^{2} = 0.08 \times (0.880)^{2} = 0.08 \times 0.7744 = 0.06195 \; \text{mol/(L \cdot min)}$$

**$-r_{A} = 0.0620 \; \text{mol/(L \cdot min)}$** (3 marks).

**Step 2:** Compute total rate of A consumption in the reactor.

$$\text{Total rate} = -r_{A} \cdot V = 0.06195 \times 50 = 3.098 \; \text{mol/min}$$

**Total rate of A consumption = 3.098 mol/min** (2 marks).

**Step 3:** Cross-check via molar balance.

$$F_{A0} - F_{A} = v_{0}(C_{A0} - C_{A}) = 5 \times (1.5 - 0.880) = 5 \times 0.620 = 3.10 \; \text{mol/min} \;\checkmark$$

(1 mark for verification, 1 mark for correct units).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalise heavily (2 to 3 marks lost per error):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the assumption of perfect mixing** in the derivation. Examiners expect you to explicitly state "due to perfect mixing, the rate is uniform throughout V" before plugging in $-r_{A}$ at exit.
> 2. **Mixing up the PFR and CSTR design equations.** PFR uses $\int dC_{A}/-r_{A}$ (integral form); CSTR uses algebraic $X/(-r_{A})$ (no integration).
> 3. **Sign convention errors.** Rate of disappearance is $-r_{A}$ (positive value). Writing $r_{A}$ alone will give a negative sign in your final answer — a guaranteed 1-mark cut.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency in numericals.** Always check $k$'s units match the reaction order assumed.
> 5. **Quadratic root selection in 2nd-order problems** — always discard the root that gives $X > 1$.
> 6. **In viva:** Students often confuse $\tau$ (space time) with **residence time distribution** — these are different concepts. $\tau$ is the mean; RTD describes the spread.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
>
> - [ ] The CSTR design equation is **algebraic**, not integral — the simplest reactor equation in CRE.
> - [ ] The rate $-r_{A}$ is **always evaluated at exit conditions** $C_{A,\text{exit}} = C_{A0}(1-X)$.
> - [ ] **Space time** $\tau = V/v_{0}$; **space velocity** $SV = 1/\tau$.
> - [ ] **1st-order CSTR closed form:** $X = k\tau / (1 + k\tau)$.
> - [ ] **2nd-order CSTR:** leads to a quadratic in $X$ — solve carefully and reject $X > 1$.
> - [ ] **Zero-order CSTR:** $X = k\tau / C_{A0}$, valid only up to $X = 1$ (after which $C_A = 0$).
> - [ ] For a given $V$ and feed, **varying $v_{0}$** (and hence $\tau$) is the standard lab technique to obtain multiple $X$ data points.
> - [ ] **Levenspiel plot** ($1/(-r_{A})$ vs $1/X$) gives a straight line through the origin with slope $V/F_{A0}$.
> - [ ] **CSTR is always larger (or equal) than a PFR** for the same conversion in positive-order kinetics — a key viva point.
> - [ ] The isothermal assumption **decouples** the energy balance; the CSTR problem reduces to a single algebraic material balance.
> - [ ] For kinetics $-r_A = k C_{A}^{n}$: the **unified design form** is $\tau = X / [kC_{A0}^{n-1}(1-X)^{n}]$.
> - [ ] For an **autocatalytic reaction** ($A + B \to 2B$ type), a CSTR can outperform a PFR — a classic KTU trick question.
> - [ ] **Lab procedure mantra:** "Set $T$ → Set $v_0$ → Wait $3\tau$ → Sample → Measure $C_A$ → Compute $X$ → Plot."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RTD Studies in a Packed Bed Reactor
RTD Studies in CSTR
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL507 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** RTD Studies in CSTR

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Residence Time Distribution (RTD)** in a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) is a probability density function that describes the amount of time individual fluid elements (or molecules) spend inside the reactor from the moment of entry to the moment of exit. It quantifies the **deviation of real reactor flow from ideal flow patterns** and is a fundamental diagnostic tool in Chemical Reaction Engineering (CRE).

For an **ideal CSTR**, the contents are assumed to be perfectly mixed, resulting in an exponential distribution of residence times. The **Exit Age Distribution Function, E(t)**, is mathematically expressed as:

$$E(t) = \frac{1}{\tau} e^{-t/\tau}$$

where the **Mean Residence Time**, **τ (tau)**, is defined as:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{Q}$$

Here, **V** is the effective reactor volume (in **m³** or **L**) and **Q** is the steady-state volumetric flow rate (in **m³/s** or **L/s**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Toll Booth Analogy 🛣️**
>
> Imagine 100 cars entering a toll plaza where some booths are open. Each car takes a different time to pass:
> - **Some cars** zip through quickly (short residence time)
> - **Some cars** get stuck behind slow drivers (long residence time)
> - **Average time** all cars spend in the plaza = Mean Residence Time (τ)
>
> The **RTD curve** is like a histogram plotting "how many cars left after exactly *t* minutes." For an **ideal CSTR** (perfect mixing), most cars leave quickly, but a few stragglers linger for a long time — giving an **exponential decay** shape. For a **plug flow reactor (PFR)**, every car spends exactly the same time → a single sharp spike.

## 1.3 Why RTD Matters in CRE

| Aspect | Significance |
|--------|--------------|
| **Reactor Diagnostics** | Identifies dead zones, channeling, bypassing |
| **Conversion Prediction** | Real conversion ≠ ideal conversion due to RTD |
| **Scale-up** | Critical for translating lab results to plant scale |
| **Troubleshooting** | Detects malfunctioning mixers, fouling, or blockages |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 The Concept of "Age" in a Reactor

Every fluid element inside a reactor has an **age** — the time elapsed since it entered. Two distributions are used:

1. **Internal Age Distribution, I(t)** — describes ages of fluid *inside* the reactor at time t
2. **Exit Age Distribution, E(t)** — describes ages of fluid *leaving* the reactor at time t

For an ideal CSTR operating at steady state:

$$E(t) = I(t) = \frac{1}{\tau} e^{-t/\tau}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The form of E(t) for an ideal CSTR is derived from a first-order washout process. If you suddenly stop the feed of a tracer, the tracer concentration decays as $C(t) = C_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ — this is the same exponential decay law governing RTD.

### 2.1.2 The Three RTD Functions

| Function | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|------------------|
| **Exit Age Density** | E(t) | Fraction of exit stream with age between t and t+dt |
| **Cumulative Distribution** | F(t) | Fraction of exit stream with age **less than** t |
| **Washout Function** | W(t) | Fraction of exit stream with age **greater than** t |

The mathematical relationship between them:

$$F(t) = \int_0^t E(t') \, dt' = 1 - e^{-t/\tau}$$

$$W(t) = 1 - F(t) = e^{-t/\tau}$$
