Doubling either doubles the *exponent*, not the *intensity reduction*. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Local Rate of Photon Absorption (LVRPA) **Step 1 — Define absorption coefficient:** Let $\mu$ be the linear absorption coefficient (units of m$^{-1}$), related to the molar absorptivity $\alpha$ by $\mu = \alpha C$. **Step 2 — Compute the absorbed intensity in slab $dL$:** From the Beer–Lambert result, the intensity lost in traversing a thin slab of thickness $dL$ is: $$dI_{abs} = -dI = \mu \, I \, dL = \mu \, I_0 \, e^{-\mu L} \, dL$$ **Step 3 — Recognize this as the local volumetric photon absorption rate (per unit volume):** Dividing by the volume of the slab (area $\times dL$), we obtain the LVRPA: $$I_a(L) = \mu \, I_0 \, e^{-\mu L}$$ **Step 4 — Average over a well-mixed reactor volume:** For an annular reactor, the average LVRPA is: $$\bar{I}_a = \dfrac{I_0}{V} \int_{0}^{L} \mu \, e^{-\mu L} \, dL = \dfrac{I_0}{V}\!\left(1 - e^{-\mu L}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Combine with quantum yield to get the photochemical rate:** $$-r_A = \Phi \cdot \bar{I}_a = \dfrac{\Phi \, I_0}{V}\!\left(1 - e^{-\mu L}\right)$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Energy of One Einstein of Photons **Step 1 — Energy of a single photon:** $$E_{photon} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$$ **Step 2 — Multiply by Avogadro's number to get energy per mole (one Einstein):** $$E_{einstein} = N_A \cdot \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values for $\lambda = 253.7$ nm (low-pressure Hg lamp):** $$E_{einstein} = \dfrac{(6.022 \times 10^{23})(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3.0 \times 10^{8})}{253.7 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$E_{einstein} = \dfrac{1.197 \times 10^{-1}}{2.537 \times 10^{-7}}$$ $$E_{einstein} = 4.718 \times 10^{5} \, \text{J/Einstein} = 471.8 \, \text{kJ/Einstein}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This value (~472 kJ/mol) is **comparable to bond dissociation energies** of many organic bonds (C–C ~ 347 kJ/mol, O–H ~ 460 kJ/mol). This is precisely why **UV light at 254 nm can directly break chemical bonds**, while visible light cannot. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Sample KTU-Style Problem) **Problem:** A 125 W low-pressure mercury UV lamp ($\lambda = 254$ nm) irradiates 1.0 L of an oxalic acid solution (initial concentration = 0.05 M) in the presence of uranyl sulfate catalyst. After 30 minutes, 12% of the oxalic acid is decomposed. Calculate the **quantum yield** $\Phi$. **Given:** - Lamp power $P = 125$ W, $\lambda = 254 \times 10^{-9}$ m - Reactor volume $V = 1.0$ L = $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m$^3$ - $t = 30$ min = 1800 s - Conversion $X_A = 0.12$ **Step 1 — Moles of oxalic acid decomposed:** $$\Delta n_A = C_{A0} \cdot V \cdot X_A = (0.05)(1.0)(0.12) = 0.006 \, \text{mol}$$ **Step 2 — Total photon energy emitted by lamp in 1800 s:** $$E_{total} = P \cdot t = (125)(1800) = 2.25 \times 10^{5} \, \text{J}$$ **Step 3 — Energy of one Einstein of photons at 254 nm:** $$E_{einstein} = \dfrac{N_A h c}{\lambda} = \dfrac{(6.022 \times 10^{23})(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3.0 \times 10^{8})}{254 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$E_{einstein} \approx 4.712 \times 10^{5} \, \text{J/Einstein}$$ **Step 4 — Total Einsteins of photons emitted:** $$n_{photon} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{E_{einstein}} = \dfrac{2.25 \times 10^{5}}{4.712 \times 10^{5}} = 0.4775 \, \text{Einstein}$$ **Step 5 — Quantum yield (assuming all photons are absorbed):** $$\Phi = \dfrac{\text{moles reacted}}{\text{moles of photons absorbed}} = \dfrac{0.006}{0.4775} = 0.0126 \approx 0.013$$ > [!TIP] > **A $\Phi$ value less than 1** is the norm for most photochemical reactions. A value greater than 1 implies a **chain reaction** (e.g., photolysis of $\text{Cl}_2$ followed by radical chain chlorination of methane). For the uranyl-oxalate actinometer, the accepted value is $\Phi \approx 0.5$–$0.6$. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Operating Variables on Rate | Variable | Effect on $-r_A$ | Reason | |---|---|---| | ↑ Lamp power $P$ | ↑ Increases linearly | More photons per second, more excitations | | ↑ Concentration $C$ | ↑ but asymptotes | More absorbers, but $e^{-\alpha C L} \to 0$ at high $C$ (optically thick) | | ↑ Path length $L$ | ↑ but asymptotes | More reaction volume, but inner zones starved of photons | | ↑ Wavelength $\lambda$ | ↓ Decreases | $E_{photon} = hc/\lambda$ decreases | | ↑ Temperature | Slight ↑ | Mostly affects dark step, not the photonic step | | ↑ Catalyst (sensitizer) | ↑ Increases | More efficient absorption and energy transfer | > [!WARNING] > **Common student mistake:** Treating the photochemical rate as directly proportional to concentration. This is only true in the *dilute limit* (when $\alpha C L \ll 1$, so $e^{-\alpha C L} \approx 1 - \alpha C L$). At high concentrations, the medium becomes **optically opaque** and the inner regions receive essentially zero photons. This is the *screening effect*. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a UV Photo Reactor System The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete functional architecture of a typical laboratory-scale UV photo reactor setup, mapping the interactions between the major subsystems. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[Power Supply Unit
220V AC to Lamp Ballast] --> nodeB[UV Lamp
Mercury Vapor 254nm] nodeB --> nodeC[Quartz Sleeve
UV Transparent Barrier] nodeC --> nodeD[Annular Reaction Zone
Reactant + Sensitizer] nodeD --> nodeE[Stirrer or Recirculation Pump
Ensures Mixing] nodeD --> nodeF[Cooling Jacket
Temperature Control] nodeG[Feed Reservoir
Reactant Solution] --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeH[Sampling Port
Time-wise Aliquots] nodeH --> nodeI[Analytical Unit
Titration / Spectrophotometer] nodeI --> nodeJ[Data Acquisition
Concentration vs Time] nodeJ --> nodeK[Kinetic Analysis
Rate, Order, Quantum Yield] ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow: Photochemical Reaction Sequence The following flow chart shows the sequence of physical and chemical events inside the reactor, from photon emission to product formation. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeL[UV Lamp Emits Photon
h nu at 254 nm] --> nodeM[Photon Traverses
Quartz Sleeve] nodeM --> nodeN[Photon Enters
Reacting Medium] nodeN --> nodeO{Beer Lambert
Absorption} nodeO -->|Absorbed| nodeP[Molecule Excited
M to M star] nodeO -->|Transmitted| nodeQ[Photon Escapes
Wasted Energy] nodeP --> nodeR[Chemical Reaction
Bond Cleavage or Rearrangement] nodeR --> nodeS[Product Formation
P plus Q] nodeP --> nodeT[Deactivation
Fluorescence or Heat] nodeS --> nodeU[Sample and Analyze
Determine Conversion] ``` ## 4.3 Reactor Type Comparison Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeV[UV Photo Reactor Types] --> nodeW[Annular Reactor
Lamp at Center] nodeV --> nodeX[Immersion Well Reactor
Lamp in Quartz Thimble] nodeV --> nodeY[Falling Film Reactor
Thin Film on Wall] nodeV --> nodeZ[Micro Reactor
Short Path High Intensity] nodeW --> nodeAA[Best for
Lab Scale Kinetics] nodeX --> nodeBB[Best for
Quantum Yield Studies] nodeY --> nodeCC[Best for
Gas Liquid Systems] nodeZ --> nodeDD[Best for
Modern Flow Chemistry] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Practical Note:** The KTU lab most commonly employs an **annular photo reactor** with a centrally placed low-pressure mercury lamp housed inside a quartz sleeve. The annular space between the sleeve and the outer glass wall contains the reacting solution, with samples drawn from a side port at known time intervals for titration (typically against standardized $\text{KMnO}_4$ for oxalic acid). ## 4.4 Energy Cascade in a UV Photo Reactor ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeEE[Electrical Energy Input
125 W at 220V AC] --> nodeFF[Ballast Losses
Approximately 10 to 15 percent] nodeFF --> nodeGG[UV Photons Emitted
Approximately 30 to 40 percent of Input] nodeGG --> nodeHH[Photons Reaching Solution
After Quartz Attenuation] nodeHH --> nodeII[Photons Absorbed by Reactant
Beer Lambert] nodeII --> nodeJJ[Photons Causing Productive Excitation
Approximately 10 to 30 percent] nodeJJ --> nodeKK[Net Chemical Conversion
Quantum Yield Times Absorbed] ``` > [!TIP] > **Why is overall efficiency so low?** The cascade above shows that typically less than **5%** of the input electrical energy ends up as useful chemical conversion. This is a key reason why UV photo reactors are designed with high $\Phi$ reactions and high photon-capture geometries. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define quantum yield in a photochemical reaction. State its typical range for a chain vs. non-chain photochemical reaction.** **Model Answer:** Quantum yield ($\Phi$) is defined as the ratio of the number of molecules of a reactant consumed (or product formed) to the number of photons absorbed by the reacting system: $$\Phi = \dfrac{\text{moles of reactant consumed}}{\text{moles of photons absorbed}}$$ - For **non-chain photochemical reactions** (e.g., direct photolysis): $0 < \Phi \leq 1$. - For **chain photochemical reactions** (e.g., photochlorination of methane): $\Phi \gg 1$, often in the range $10^3$ to $10^6$, because one photon initiates a long radical chain. > **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **State the Beer–Lambert Law. Why is a quartz sleeve used to house the UV lamp inside the photo reactor instead of ordinary glass?** **Model Answer:** The Beer–Lambert Law states that the intensity of monochromatic light transmitted through an absorbing medium decreases exponentially with the path length and concentration: $$I = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha C L}$$ A **quartz sleeve** is used because: 1. Ordinary soda-lime glass strongly absorbs UV radiation below ~350 nm (it blocks UV-C entirely). 2. Fused quartz is transparent down to ~200 nm, allowing 254 nm UV-C photons to pass into the reaction medium. 3. Quartz also provides a thermal barrier between the hot lamp surface (~40–60 °C) and the cool reacting solution. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Q3 (A).** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme] **(a)** Derive an expression for the **local volumetric rate of photon absorption (LVRPA)** in an annular UV photo reactor and hence obtain the photochemical reaction rate equation. **(b)** A UV lamp of 8 W nominal power at $\lambda = 254$ nm is used to study the photolysis of hydrogen peroxide ($\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{OH}^\bullet$) in a 500 mL batch reactor. If the quantum yield is 0.5 and 30% of emitted photons are absorbed, calculate the **initial rate of $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ decomposition** in mol/L·min. ### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] The Beer–Lambert law gives the intensity at depth $L$ from the lamp surface: $$I(L) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha C L}$$ The intensity absorbed in an infinitesimal slab of thickness $dL$ is: $$dI_{abs} = -dI = \alpha C \, I_0 \, e^{-\alpha C L} \, dL$$ Dividing by the slab volume (area $A \cdot dL$) gives the **LVRPA**: $$I_a(L) = \dfrac{dI_{abs}}{A \, dL} = \alpha C \, I_0 \, e^{-\alpha C L} = \mu \, I_0 \, e^{-\mu L}$$ Averaging over the annular path length $L_0$: $$\bar{I}_a = \dfrac{1}{V}\int_0^{L_0} \mu \, I_0 \, e^{-\mu L} A \, dL = \dfrac{I_0 \, A}{V}\!\left(1 - e^{-\mu L_0}\right)$$ The photochemical rate (per unit volume) is the product of quantum yield and LVRPA: $$-r_A = \Phi \cdot \bar{I}_a = \dfrac{\Phi \, I_0 \, A}{V}\!\left(1 - e^{-\mu L_0}\right)$$ ### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Energy emitted per second by lamp:** $$P = 8 \, \text{W} = 8 \, \text{J/s}$$ **Step 2 — Energy of one Einstein at 254 nm:** $$E_{einstein} = \dfrac{N_A \, h \, c}{\lambda} = \dfrac{(6.022 \times 10^{23})(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{254 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$E_{einstein} \approx 4.712 \times 10^5 \, \text{J/Einstein}$$ **Step 3 — Photon emission rate (in Einstein/s):** $$\dot{n}_{emit} = \dfrac{8}{4.712 \times 10^5} = 1.698 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Einstein/s}$$ **Step 4 — Photon absorption rate (30% absorption):** $$\dot{n}_{abs} = 0.30 \times 1.698 \times 10^{-5} = 5.094 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{Einstein/s}$$ **Step 5 — Rate of $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ consumption (using quantum yield):** $$\dot{n}_{H_2O_2} = \Phi \times \dot{n}_{abs} = 0.5 \times 5.094 \times 10^{-6} = 2.547 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{mol/s}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to mol/L·min:** $$-r_{H_2O_2} = \dfrac{2.547 \times 10^{-6} \times 60}{0.5} = 3.056 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{mol/(L·min)}$$ **Final Answer:** $-r_{H_2O_2} \approx 3.06 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/(L·min). --- > **Q3 (B).** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice] **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working of an annular UV photo reactor** used in the KTU chemical reaction engineering lab. **(b)** In a uranyl-sulfate catalyzed photodecomposition of oxalic acid experiment, the following data is obtained at constant lamp intensity: $C_0 = 0.04$ M, $C_{10} = 0.032$ M, $C_{20} = 0.0256$ M, $C_{30} = 0.0205$ M (subscripts denote time in minutes). Determine the **order of the reaction** and the **apparent rate constant**. ### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Construction:** - A **low-pressure mercury vapor lamp** (8–125 W) is mounted vertically at the center, emitting predominantly at 254 nm. - The lamp is encased in a **cylindrical quartz sleeve** (transparent to UV-C) which isolates the lamp from the reaction mixture. - The annular space between the quartz sleeve and the outer glass jacket holds the **reacting solution** (oxalic acid + uranyl sulfate catalyst). - A **stirrer** or **recirculation pump** ensures uniform mixing. - A **cooling jacket** surrounds the outer wall to remove heat from the lamp and maintain isothermal conditions. - A **sampling port** is provided on the side for periodic withdrawal of aliquots. **Working:** 1. The lamp emits UV photons at 254 nm. 2. Photons pass through the quartz sleeve and enter the annular reacting solution. 3. Uranyl ions ($\text{UO}_2^{2+}$) absorb the photons and get excited to $(\text{UO}_2^{2+})^*$. 4. The excited uranyl ion transfers energy to oxalic acid, decomposing it: $$\text{H}_2\text{C}_2\text{O}_4 \xrightarrow{\text{UV}, \, \text{UO}_2^{2+}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{HCOOH}$$ 5. Aliquots are withdrawn at fixed times, and residual oxalic acid is titrated against standardized $\text{KMnO}_4$. ### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Compute the concentration ratios:** | Time (min) | $C$ (M) | $\ln C$ | $1/C$ | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0.0400 | $-3.219$ | 25.00 | | 10 | 0.0320 | $-3.442$ | 31.25 | | 20 | 0.0256 | $-3.665$ | 39.06 | | 30 | 0.0205 | $-3.887$ | 48.78 | **Step 2 — Test for first order:** Check if $\ln C$ vs. $t$ is linear. Differences in $\ln C$ over equal 10-min intervals: - $\Delta(\ln C)_{0 \to 10} = -3.442 - (-3.219) = -0.223$ - $\Delta(\ln C)_{10 \to 20} = -3.665 - (-3.442) = -0.223$ - $\Delta(\ln C)_{20 \to 30} = -3.887 - (-3.665) = -0.222$ The differences are essentially **constant** ($\approx -0.223$), confirming **first-order kinetics**. **Step 3 — Calculate the apparent first-order rate constant:** $$k' = -\dfrac{\Delta(\ln C)}{\Delta t} = -\dfrac{(-0.223)}{10} = 0.0223 \, \text{min}^{-1}$$ **Verification using two-point formula:** $$k' = \dfrac{1}{t}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_t}\right) = \dfrac{1}{30}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.0400}{0.0205}\right) = \dfrac{0.668}{30} = 0.0223 \, \text{min}^{-1}$$ **Final Answer:** **First order**, $k' \approx 0.0223$ min$^{-1}$. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students commonly lose marks in UV photo reactor problems:** > > 1. **Unit conversion of wavelength:** Forgetting to convert nm to m. The error introduces a factor of $10^9$ in photon energy, which is a guaranteed 3-mark deduction. > 2. **Confusing "incident" vs. "absorbed" photons:** Quantum yield uses *absorbed* photons, not *emitted* photons. If the problem states "30% of emitted photons are absorbed", you must multiply by 0.30. > 3. **Mixing up Einstein and Joule:** One Einstein = $N_A$ photons, not one photon. Many students divide $P$ by $E_{photon}$ (per photon) instead of $E_{einstein}$ (per mole of photons), inflating the answer by $6 \times 10^{23}$. > 4. **Ignoring the exponential in Beer–Lambert:** Writing $I_a = \mu I_0$ instead of $I_a = \mu I_0 e^{-\mu L}$ is valid *only* in the very dilute limit. In most KTU problems, the exponential form is required. > 5. **Reporting rate in wrong units:** The expected unit for a lab-scale reactor is mol/(L·s) or mol/(L·min), not mol/s (which is an extensive quantity, not an intensive rate). > 6. **Skipping the data-table step:** Always tabulate the experimental $t$, $C$, $\ln C$, and $1/C$ before deciding the order — examiners reward *methodology* (3 marks are reserved for the testing procedure alone). ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — UV Photo Reactor** > > ☐ **Beer–Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha C L}$ — the master equation of photochemistry. > > ☐ **Photon energy:** $E = hc/\lambda$; remember to convert $\lambda$ to **meters**. > > ☐ **One Einstein** = $N_A$ photons = $N_A hc / \lambda$ joules. > > ☐ **Quantum yield** $\Phi$ = (moles reacted) / (moles photons absorbed). $\Phi > 1$ implies a chain reaction. > > ☐ **LVRPA:** $I_a = \mu I_0 e^{-\mu L}$; averaged over reactor volume. > > ☐ **Photochemical rate:** $-r_A = \Phi \cdot \bar{I}_a$. > > ☐ **Quartz sleeve** is mandatory — glass blocks UV-C below 350 nm. > > ☐ **Order determination** uses $\ln C$ vs. $t$ (1st order), $1/C$ vs. $t$ (2nd order), $C$ vs. $t$ (0th order). > > ☐ **Uranyl oxalate actinometer** is the standard chemical dosimeter for UV-C at 254 nm. > > ☐ **Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)** = UV + $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ / $\text{O}_3$ / $\text{TiO}_2$ — a favourite KTU essay topic. > > ☐ **Optical thickness screening:** At high $C$ or $L$, the inner reactor zones become photon-starved, and increasing $C$ further gives *no* additional rate benefit. > > ☐ **Einstein's photochemical equivalence law:** Each photon absorbed activates one molecule. This is the foundation of $\Phi$ being bounded above by 1 in non-chain reactions.

topics.title: Determination of Activation Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL507 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Activation Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In chemical reaction engineering, **Activation Energy ($E_a$)** is defined as the minimum threshold energy that reactant molecules must possess in order to undergo a successful chemical transformation into products during a collision. Mathematically, it represents the energy barrier separating the reactants (initial state) from the products (final state) along the reaction coordinate.

The Swedish chemist **Svante Arrhenius (1889)** established the quantitative relationship between the rate constant of a chemical reaction and the absolute temperature through the **Arrhenius Equation**:

$$k = A \cdot e^{\left(\dfrac{-E_a}{RT}\right)}$$

Where:
- $k$ = Rate constant of the reaction
- $A$ = Pre-exponential (frequency) factor
- $E_a$ = Activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The determination of activation energy in **PCCHL507** is a quantitative experiment where students experimentally obtain rate constants ($k$) at multiple temperatures (typically 3–4 different temperatures in the range of 30 °C–60 °C) and then use the linearized Arrhenius equation to compute $E_a$ and $A$.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the bottom of a hill and want to roll a heavy stone ball to the other side. The ball has enough kinetic energy at the bottom, but it **cannot cross the hill** unless you give it an extra push — that extra push represents the **activation energy**.

A more vivid real-world analogy:
- **Reaction = Lighting a campfire.** The matchstick must reach a certain temperature (activation energy) before the wood starts burning. Once the fire starts, the released heat sustains the reaction.
- **Reactant molecules = Cars parked in a valley.** To reach the destination city on the other side of a mountain range, the cars must climb up and over the mountain pass. The height of the pass is the **activation energy** $E_a$.
- **Higher $E_a$ = Higher mountain pass** → fewer molecules have sufficient energy → slower reaction.
- **Higher $T$** → more molecules gain the threshold energy → faster reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> Even exothermic reactions require activation energy. The energy "released" by a reaction is the **enthalpy change ($\Delta H$)**, which is *different* from $E_a$. The activation energy is what must be **invested first**, while $\Delta H$ is what is **ultimately released or absorbed**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **1.987** | cal/(mol·K) |
| Reference Temperature | $T$ | 298.15 (often used as reference) | K |
| Typical $E_a$ range (chemical rxns) | $E_a$ | 40 – 200 | kJ/mol |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Reactions with $E_a < 40$ kJ/mol proceed rapidly even at room temperature (e.g., neutralization of strong acid–base), while reactions with $E_a > 100$ kJ/mol require significant heating or catalysis (e.g., cracking of hydrocarbons, Haber–Bosch ammonia synthesis).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The Arrhenius equation captures the temperature sensitivity of a chemical reaction. Let us dissect it logically:

1. **The Boltzmann Energy Distribution:** At any temperature $T$, only a fraction of molecules possess energy $\geq E_a$. This fraction is given by the Boltzmann factor:
$$f = e^{\left(\dfrac{-E_a}{RT}\right)}$$

2. **The Frequency Factor $A$:** Represents the total number of collisions per unit time with proper orientation, regardless of energy.

3. **The Rate Constant $k$:** Product of $A$ and the fraction of successful collisions:
$$k = A \cdot e^{\left(\dfrac{-E_a}{RT}\right)}$$

4. **Linearization:** Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:
$$\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$$

This is the equation of a straight line: $y = c + m \cdot x$
- $y = \ln k$
- $x = 1/T$
- Slope $m = -E_a/R$
- Intercept $c = \ln A$

A plot of $\ln k$ versus $1/T$ yields a **straight line with negative slope**, known as the **Arrhenius Plot**.

---

### 2.2 Why and How — Engineering Significance

- **"Why" measure $E_a$ experimentally:** Every reaction has a unique $E_a$. Knowing it allows chemical engineers to predict reaction rates at industrial temperatures without running the actual reaction.
- **"How" the Arrhenius plot works:** Larger $E_a$ → steeper negative slope → reaction is more temperature-sensitive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (Q₁₀ Rule):** For many reactions, the rate constant approximately **doubles for every 10 °C rise** in temperature. This corresponds to $E_a \approx 50$ kJ/mol at room temperature.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula | Description | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k = A \cdot e^{(-E_a / RT)}$ | Arrhenius Equation (Exponential Form) | Direct rate constant calculation |
| 2 | $\ln k = \ln A - (E_a / R)(1/T)$ | Linearized Arrhenius Equation | Plotting Arrhenius graph |
| 3 | $\log_{10} k = \log_{10} A - (E_a / 2.303R)(1/T)$ | Base-10 Linearized Form | When common log is preferred |
| 4 | $\ln (k_2 / k_1) = (E_a / R) \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right)$ | Two-Point Arrhenius Form | Calculation between two temperatures |
| 5 | $E_a = \dfrac{R \cdot \ln(k_2 / k_1)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)}$ | Direct $E_a$ Calculation | Numerical problems |
| 6 | $\text{Slope} = -E_a / R$ | Arrhenius Plot Slope | Graphical determination |
| 7 | $A = k \cdot e^{(E_a / RT)}$ | Pre-exponential Factor | When $k$ and $E_a$ known |
| 8 | $\dfrac{d(\ln k)}{dT} = \dfrac{E_a}{RT^2}$ | Differential Form | Derived from Arrhenius eq. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** In the two-point form, **larger $k$ corresponds to larger $T$**. So if $T_2 > T_1$, then $k_2 > k_1$, and $\ln(k_2 / k_1) > 0$.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Reactor Design:** Used to predict conversion in CSTRs, PFRs, and batch reactors at industrial temperatures.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Shelf-life predictions (drug degradation kinetics).
- **Petrochemical:** Cracking, reforming, and polymerization rate predictions.
- **Food Industry:** Sterilization and pasteurization process optimization.
- **Catalysis:** Catalyst development relies on lowering $E_a$ to speed up reactions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Linearized Arrhenius Equation

**Starting Point:** Arrhenius proposed empirically that the rate constant depends on temperature as:

$$k = A \cdot e^{\left(\dfrac{-E_a}{RT}\right)}$$

**Step 1:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides.

$$\ln k = \ln \left[ A \cdot e^{\left(\dfrac{-E_a}{RT}\right)} \right]$$

**Step 2:** Apply the logarithm property $\ln(a \cdot b) = \ln a + \ln b$.

$$\ln k = \ln A + \ln \left[ e^{\left(\dfrac{-E_a}{RT}\right)} \right]$$

**Step 3:** Apply the property $\ln(e^x) = x$.

$$\ln k = \ln A + \left( \dfrac{-E_a}{RT} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange in the form $y = mx + c$.

$$\ln k = -\dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + \ln A$$

This is the final linearized form used for plotting.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Two-Point Form

**Step 1:** Write the linearized equation for temperature $T_1$.

$$\ln k_1 = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T_1}$$

**Step 2:** Write the linearized equation for temperature $T_2$.

$$\ln k_2 = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T_2}$$

**Step 3:** Subtract Equation 1 from Equation 2.

$$\ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = -\dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T_2} + \dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T_1}$$

**Step 4:** Apply logarithm subtraction rule and factor out $E_a/R$.

$$\ln \left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Solve explicitly for $E_a$.

$$E_a = \dfrac{R \cdot \ln(k_2 / k_1)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem (Typical KTU ESE Question):**

The rate constant of a first-order reaction is $1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$ at 35 °C and $3.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$ at 50 °C. Calculate the activation energy $E_a$ in kJ/mol and the pre-exponential factor $A$.

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 35 + 273 = 308 \text{ K}$
- $T_2 = 50 + 273 = 323 \text{ K}$
- $k_1 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$
- $k_2 = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$

**Step 1:** Apply the two-point Arrhenius formula.

$$E_a = \dfrac{R \cdot \ln(k_2 / k_1)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$E_a = \dfrac{8.314 \times \ln(3.0 \times 10^{-3} / 1.5 \times 10^{-3})}{(1/308) - (1/323)}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator.

$$\ln(2.0) = 0.6931$$

$$8.314 \times 0.6931 = 5.7625 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator.

$$\dfrac{1}{308} - \dfrac{1}{323} = 0.003247 - 0.003096 = 0.000151 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5:** Divide numerator by denominator.

$$E_a = \dfrac{5.7625}{0.000151} = 38162 \text{ J/mol} \approx 38.16 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate pre-exponential factor $A$ using $k = A \cdot e^{(-E_a / RT)}$.

$$A = k_1 \cdot e^{(E_a / RT_1)}$$

**Step 7:** Compute the exponent.

$$\dfrac{E_a}{R T_1} = \dfrac{38162}{8.314 \times 308} = \dfrac{38162}{2560.7} = 14.904$$

**Step 8:** Compute $e^{14.904}$.

$$e^{14.904} \approx 2.948 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 9:** Final calculation for $A$.

$$A = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.948 \times 10^{6} = 4422 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Final Answer:**
- $E_a \approx 38.16 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $A \approx 4.42 \times 10^{3} \text{ s}^{-1}$

---

### 3.4 Graphical Analysis Example

Suppose the following data is obtained experimentally:

| S.No. | Temperature $T$ (K) | Rate Constant $k$ (s⁻¹) | $1/T$ (K⁻¹) | $\ln k$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 303 | $2.50 \times 10^{-4}$ | $3.300 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-8.294$ |
| 2 | 313 | $5.40 \times 10^{-4}$ | $3.195 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-7.524$ |
| 3 | 323 | $1.15 \times 10^{-3}$ | $3.096 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-6.768$ |
| 4 | 333 | $2.30 \times 10^{-3}$ | $3.003 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-6.075$ |

**Step 1:** Plot $\ln k$ versus $1/T$.

**Step 2:** Determine slope from best-fit line.

$$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{\Delta(\ln k)}{\Delta(1/T)} = \dfrac{-6.075 - (-8.294)}{3.003 \times 10^{-3} - 3.300 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{2.219}{-2.97 \times 10^{-4}} = -7471 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $E_a$.

$$E_a = -R \cdot \text{Slope} = -8.314 \times (-7471) = 62,108 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$E_a \approx 62.11 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $A$ from intercept.

Intercept $\approx 16.36$

$$A = e^{16.36} = 1.27 \times 10^{7} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Experimental Procedure

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the procedural workflow for the determination of activation energy, mapping out each functional block and the information flow between them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A: Prepare Reactants and Standardize Solutions] --> B[Step B: Charge Reactor with Measured Volumes]
    B --> C[Step C: Equilibrate in Thermostatic Water Bath at T1]
    C --> D[Step D: Initiate Reaction and Note Start Time]
    D --> E[Step E: Withdraw Aliquots at Fixed Time Intervals]
    E --> F[Step F: Quench Reaction and Titrate Samples]
    F --> G[Step G: Compute Concentration vs Time Data]
    G --> H[Step H: Determine Rate Constant k1 at T1]
    H --> I{Step I: Repeat for All Temperatures?}
    I -->|Yes, Next Temperature| J[Step J: Reset Bath to T2 and Repeat Procedure]
    J --> C
    I -->|No, All Done| K[Step K: Tabulate k Values vs Temperatures]
    K --> L[Step L: Plot ln k versus 1/T Arrhenius Plot]
    L --> M[Step M: Determine Slope from Best Fit Line]
    M --> N[Step N: Calculate Ea equals minus R times Slope]
    N --> O[Step O: Compute A from Intercept]
    O --> P[Step P: Report Final Ea and A with Units]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Decision Logic for Data Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Experimental Data: Concentration vs Time at each T] --> B[Identify Reaction Order]
    B --> C{First Order?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Use Integrated First Order Law to find k]
    C -->|No| E{Second Order?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Use Second Order Integrated Law to find k]
    E -->|No| G[Use Zero Order Integrated Law to find k]
    D --> H[Collect k1, k2, k3, k4]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute 1 over T and ln k for each T]
    I --> J[Plot ln k versus 1 over T]
    J --> K[Compute Slope and Intercept]
    K --> L[Ea equals minus R times Slope]
    K --> M[A equals exp of Intercept]
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture Mapping — Rate Constant Determination Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ReactantModule[Reactant Preparation Stage]
        R1[Measure Volume of Ester] --> R2[Measure Volume of Acid Catalyst]
        R2 --> R3[Mix in Round Bottom Flask]
    end

    subgraph ReactionModule[Reaction Stage at Controlled T]
        R3 --> S1[Immerse in Constant T Water Bath]
        S1 --> S2[Withdraw 5 mL Aliquots Every 5 min]
        S2 --> S3[Quench in Ice Cold Water]
    end

    subgraph AnalysisModule[Analytical Stage]
        S3 --> AA1[Titrate Against Standard NaOH using Phenolphthalein]
        AA1 --> AA2[Record Burette Readings and Calculate Unreacted Acid]
        AA2 --> AA3[Compute Fraction Reacted x versus t]
    end

    subgraph ComputationModule[Computation Stage]
        AA3 --> CC1[Apply Integrated Rate Law]
        CC1 --> CC2[Determine k for Current T]
        CC2 --> CC3[Store k and T in Data Table]
    end

    CC3 --> FinalPlot[Construct Arrhenius Plot ln k vs 1 over T]
    FinalPlot --> FinalReport[Compute Ea and A and Tabulate Results]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Exam Tip:** When drawing or describing the experimental setup, always include the **thermostatic water bath**, **thermometer**, **burette/analytical setup**, and **sampling port**. Examiners specifically look for these in the viva and record.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
Define **activation energy** and write the **Arrhenius equation**. Explain the significance of the **pre-exponential factor**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Activation energy $E_a$ is the minimum amount of energy that reactant molecules must acquire so that they can undergo a successful chemical reaction to form products.

**Arrhenius Equation (1 mark):**

$$k = A \cdot e^{\left(\dfrac{-E_a}{RT}\right)}$$

**Pre-exponential Factor (1 mark):** The pre-exponential factor $A$ represents the frequency of molecular collisions per unit time with proper orientation. It is approximately independent of temperature and depends on molecular geometry and collision frequency.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
What is an **Arrhenius plot**? Mention the **slope** and **intercept** of such a plot.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** An Arrhenius plot is a graph of $\ln k$ (on Y-axis) versus $1/T$ (on X-axis). According to the linearized Arrhenius equation, this yields a straight line.

**Slope and Intercept (1.5 marks):**

$$\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$$

- **Slope** = $-E_a / R$
- **Intercept** = $\ln A$

The activation energy is computed as $E_a = -R \times \text{slope}$, and the pre-exponential factor is $A = e^{\text{intercept}}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Derive the **linearized Arrhenius equation** starting from the exponential form. Using this derivation, show how the activation energy can be determined graphically. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Start with the Arrhenius equation in exponential form:

$$k = A \cdot e^{\left(\dfrac{-E_a}{RT}\right)}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Take natural logarithm on both sides:

$$\ln k = \ln \left[ A \cdot e^{\left(\dfrac{-E_a}{RT}\right)} \right]$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply logarithm properties:

$$\ln k = \ln A + \ln \left[ e^{\left(\dfrac{-E_a}{RT}\right)} \right]$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Simplify:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Comparing with $y = mx + c$, we identify:
- $y = \ln k$
- $x = 1/T$
- Slope $m = -E_a / R$
- Intercept $c = \ln A$

The activation energy is obtained from the slope: $E_a = -R \times \text{slope}$.

---

**(b)** The rate constants for a chemical reaction are found to be $2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-1}$ at 300 K and $7.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-1}$ at 320 K. Calculate the **activation energy** and the **pre-exponential factor**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$, $k_1 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-1}$
- $T_2 = 320 \text{ K}$, $k_2 = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-1}$
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Use the two-point form:

$$E_a = \dfrac{R \cdot \ln(k_2 / k_1)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute values:

$$E_a = \dfrac{8.314 \times \ln(7.5 \times 10^{-4} / 2.5 \times 10^{-4})}{(1/300) - (1/320)}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the terms:

$$\ln(3.0) = 1.0986$$

$$\dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{320} = 0.003333 - 0.003125 = 0.000208 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Final calculation:

$$E_a = \dfrac{8.314 \times 1.0986}{0.000208} = \dfrac{9.135}{0.000208} = 43,917 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 43.92 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Calculate $A$ using $k = A \cdot e^{(-E_a / RT)}$ at $T_1$:

$$A = k_1 \cdot e^{(E_a / RT_1)} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot e^{(43917 / (8.314 \times 300))}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Compute exponent:

$$\dfrac{43917}{2494.2} = 17.608$$

$$e^{17.608} = 4.516 \times 10^{7}$$

$$A = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 4.516 \times 10^{7} = 1.129 \times 10^{4} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 1.13 \times 10^{4} \text{ s}^{-1}}$$

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **experimental procedure** for the determination of activation energy of the **acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of ethyl acetate**. Include the chemicals, apparatus, and method of analysis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Chemicals Used (1 mark):**
- Ethyl acetate (CH₃COOC₂H₅) — substrate
- Hydrochloric acid (HCl) — catalyst
- Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) — titrant for back-titration
- Phenolphthalein — indicator

**Apparatus (2 marks):**
- Thermostatic water bath (precision ±0.1 °C)
- Borosilicate round-bottom flasks with stoppers
- Burette and pipettes
- Stopwatch
- Thermometer calibrated to 0.1 °C

**Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **Preparation:** Measure known volumes of ethyl acetate and HCl of standardized concentration into separate flasks.

2. **Equilibration:** Keep both flasks in the thermostatic water bath at the first desired temperature $T_1$ (e.g., 30 °C) for about 10 minutes to attain thermal equilibrium.

3. **Initiation:** Mix the two solutions, start the stopwatch immediately, and shake thoroughly.

4. **Sampling:** Withdraw 5 mL aliquots of the reaction mixture at regular time intervals (e.g., every 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 min) using a pipette. Quench each aliquot immediately in ice-cold water to arrest the reaction.

5. **Titration:** Titrate each quenched aliquot against standard NaOH using phenolphthalein indicator to determine the unreacted HCl concentration at that instant. (Equivalently, for hydrolysis of ester with NaOH stoichiometry, similar titrimetric analysis is done.)

6. **Calculation of $k$:** Use the appropriate integrated rate law to determine the pseudo-first-order rate constant $k_1$ at temperature $T_1$.

7. **Repetition:** Repeat the entire procedure at three or four different temperatures (e.g., 35 °C, 40 °C, 45 °C, 50 °C).

8. **Plotting:** Tabulate $k$ vs $T$, then compute $\ln k$ and $1/T$. Plot $\ln k$ vs $1/T$, fit a straight line, and compute the slope.

9. **Final Computation:** $E_a = -R \times \text{slope}$, and $A = e^{\text{intercept}}$.

---

**(b)** For a certain chemical reaction, the **rate constant doubles** when the temperature changes from 300 K to 310 K. Calculate the **activation energy** of the reaction in kJ/mol. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):**
- $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$
- $T_2 = 310 \text{ K}$
- $k_2 = 2 \cdot k_1$, i.e., $k_2 / k_1 = 2$
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply the two-point Arrhenius equation:

$$\ln \left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute $k_2/k_1 = 2$:

$$\ln 2 = \dfrac{E_a}{8.314} \left( \dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{310} \right)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the terms:

$$0.6931 = \dfrac{E_a}{8.314} \cdot (0.003333 - 0.003226)$$

$$0.6931 = \dfrac{E_a}{8.314} \cdot 1.075 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Solve for $E_a$:

$$E_a = \dfrac{0.6931 \times 8.314}{1.075 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a = \dfrac{5.7625}{1.075 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{E_a = 53,605 \text{ J/mol} \approx 53.6 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Comment on result:

This value of $E_a \approx 53.6$ kJ/mol is typical for many liquid-phase reactions and confirms the "Q₁₀ ≈ 2" rule at room temperature.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PCCHL507 Exam:**
>
> 1. **Temperature Unit Error:** Using °C instead of K in the Arrhenius equation. **Always convert °C to K** by adding 273.15.
> 2. **Inverse Sign in Slope:** Forgetting the negative sign when computing $E_a$ from slope. Remember: $E_a = -R \times \text{slope}$. The slope is **negative**, so $E_a$ is **positive**.
> 3. **Order of $T$ in Two-Point Form:** Mixing up $T_1$ and $T_2$. The formula has $(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$, not $(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$.
> 4. **Units of $E_a$:** Reporting $E_a$ in J/mol instead of kJ/mol (or vice versa). Be consistent.
> 5. **Final Slope Sign:** Examiners explicitly check the sign of slope. Wrong sign = full marks lost.
> 6. **Missing Decimals:** Always carry at least 4 significant figures in intermediate steps.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Activation Energy Determination:**
>
> - **Arrhenius Equation:** $k = A \cdot e^{(-E_a / RT)}$ — exponential form.
> - **Linearized Form:** $\ln k = \ln A - (E_a / R)(1/T)$ — plot form.
> - **Two-Point Form:** $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E_a/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$ — quick numerical.
> - **Slope of Arrhenius Plot** = $-E_a / R$ (negative slope).
> - **Intercept of Arrhenius Plot** = $\ln A$.
> - **$E_a$ computation:** $E_a = -R \times \text{slope}$.
> - **$A$ computation:** $A = e^{\text{intercept}}$.
> - **Always use Kelvin** for temperature in Arrhenius calculations.
> - **Typical experiment:** Acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of ethyl acetate at 4 different temperatures (e.g., 30, 35, 40, 45 °C).
> - **Plotting:** $\ln k$ vs $1/T$ must yield a straight line with **negative slope**.
> - **Linear regression** is preferred over two-point calculation for accuracy.
> - **Sign convention:** Slope is negative, so $-R \times (\text{negative slope})$ gives positive $E_a$.
> - **Order of reaction** must be correctly identified before computing $k$.
> - **Q₁₀ rule:** $E_a \approx 50$ kJ/mol for many reactions where rate doubles every 10 °C.
> - **Units:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) gives $E_a$ in J/mol. Convert to kJ/mol by dividing by 1000.
> - **Common lab reactions:** Ester hydrolysis, saponification, sucrose inversion, decomposition of H₂O₂.
> - **Reagents:** Ethyl acetate + HCl (acid-catalyzed hydrolysis), or ethyl acetate + NaOH (saponification).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinetics of Reactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL507 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Kinetics of Reactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Kinetics of Reactions

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Chemical Reaction Kinetics** is the branch of physical chemistry and reaction engineering that quantitatively studies the **rates of chemical reactions**, the **influence of process variables** (concentration, temperature, pressure, catalyst) on these rates, and the **molecular mechanisms** by which reactants are transformed into products.

In the context of KTU Module Experiment 2, *Kinetics of Reactions* refers to the **experimental determination of the rate law parameters** — namely the **reaction order (n)**, the **rate constant (k)**, and the **activation energy (Ea)** — for a chosen homogeneous liquid-phase reaction (e.g., saponification of ethyl acetate with NaOH, hydrolysis of sucrose, or iodine clock reaction) carried out in a **batch reactor**.

The **rate of reaction** $r_A$ is formally defined as the rate of disappearance of reactant A per unit volume:

$$r_A = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt} = -\frac{dC_A}{dt}$$

For a constant-volume batch system where $V$ remains unchanged, the rate can be expressed simply in terms of concentration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome for this module (mapped to **CO2** under the 2024 NEP framework) requires the student to *“determine kinetic parameters such as reaction order, rate constant, and activation energy from laboratory batch reactor data using graphical and integral methods of analysis.”*

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍳

Imagine you are cooking **maggi noodles** in a vessel:
- The **noodles + water + masala** are the *reactants*.
- The **cooked noodles** are the *products*.
- The **rate at which raw noodles become soft** is the *reaction rate*.
- If you add **less water**, cooking slows down (low concentration → low rate).
- If you **turn up the stove flame**, cooking speeds up (high temperature → high rate).
- The **recipe card** itself is the *rate law*, telling you *how fast* and *in what proportion* the ingredients react.

Just as a chef times the cooking by repeatedly poking the noodles, a chemical engineer *samples the reactor at fixed time intervals* and measures the concentration of reactants/products to figure out how fast the reaction is proceeding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** *Kinetics* tells us **HOW FAST** a reaction proceeds. *Thermodynamics* tells us **WHETHER** the reaction can proceed at all. A reaction can be thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically so slow that it is practically unobservable (e.g., diamond → graphite).

### Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module

- **Reaction Rate Constant (k):** Units vary with order — typically $\text{L}^{n-1}\text{mol}^{1-n}\text{s}^{-1}$ where $n$ is the overall order.
- **Activation Energy (Ea):** Typically **40 – 200 kJ/mol** for most liquid-phase reactions.
- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)** — used in Arrhenius equation.
- **Half-life (t₁/₂):** Time required for reactant concentration to fall to 50% of its initial value.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Rate Law

For a single irreversible reaction:

$$\nu_A A + \nu_B B \longrightarrow \text{Products}$$

The empirical rate law is written as:

$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A^{\alpha} \cdot C_B^{\beta} \cdot C_C^{\gamma} \cdots$$

where:
- $k$ = rate constant (temperature dependent)
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = orders with respect to individual species (NOT stoichiometric coefficients in general)
- $n = \alpha + \beta + \gamma + \cdots$ = **overall order of reaction**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most important fact in chemical kinetics:** The reaction order is determined **EXPERIMENTALLY**, not from the stoichiometric equation. For example, the reaction $\text{H}_2 + \text{Br}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{HBr}$ has a complex rate law, not simply proportional to $C_{H_2} \cdot C_{Br_2}$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow in a Batch Kinetics Experiment

A typical Module 2 kinetics experiment follows this structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Reactor Setup:** Charge a temperature-controlled batch reactor (jacketed glass vessel with stirrer) with one reactant solution preheated to the desired temperature $T_1$.
- **Step 2 — Initiate Reaction:** At $t = 0$, quickly add the second reactant. Start the stopwatch.
- **Step 3 — Sampling:** Withdraw small aliquots ($\sim 2$–$5$ mL) at predetermined time intervals.
- **Step 4 — Quench & Analyze:** Immediately quench the sample (e.g., by cooling or adding inhibitor) to stop the reaction. Analyze concentration via titration, conductivity, or spectrophotometry.
- **Step 5 — Repeat:** Redo the experiment at 2–3 different temperatures (e.g., 303 K, 313 K, 323 K) and/or with different initial concentrations to isolate orders and find $E_a$.
- **Step 6 — Data Reduction:** Plot concentration vs. time data using **three classic methods**:
  - **Differential Method** (plot $\ln(-r_A)$ vs. $\ln C_A$)
  - **Integral Method** (try fit with 0th, 1st, 2nd order integrated forms)
  - **Half-life Method** (plot $\ln t_{1/2}$ vs. $\ln C_{A0}$)

## 2.3 Why and How Each Method Works

- **Integral Method — Why:** We integrate the differential rate law to get an explicit equation $C_A = f(t)$. By trial, the order that gives the **best straight-line fit** through the data is the correct one.
- **Differential Method — Why:** Take $\ln$ of $r_A = k C_A^n$ to get a linear equation $\ln r_A = \ln k + n \ln C_A$. Slope = $n$. Works for any order but requires smooth derivative data.
- **Half-life Method — Why:** $t_{1/2} \propto C_{A0}^{1-n}$ for $n \neq 1$. Taking logs gives $\ln t_{1/2} = \text{const} + (1-n)\ln C_{A0}$. Slope directly yields $n$.
- **Arrhenius Plot — Why:** $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ linearized as $\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$. Plotting $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$ gives slope $-E_a/R$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Kinetic data is the **backbone of reactor design**. Without $-r_A = f(C, T)$, an engineer cannot:
- Size an industrial reactor (CSTR volume, PFR length)
- Predict conversion vs. residence time
- Optimize temperature profiles in adiabatic reactors
- Design catalyst regeneration schedules
- Establish safe operating windows to avoid thermal runaway

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use these boxed expressions as your ready-reference during exams. Units must accompany every numerical answer.

| **#** | **Concept** | **Differential Form** | **Integrated Form (Batch)** | **Linear Plot (Y vs. X)** | **Units of k** |
|:-----:|:-----------|:---------------------|:---------------------------|:-------------------------|:---------------|
| 0 | Zero order ($n=0$) | $-r_A = k$ | $C_A = C_{A0} - kt$ | $C_A$ vs. $t$ | $\text{mol L}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 1 | First order ($n=1$) | $-r_A = kC_A$ | $\ln C_A = \ln C_{A0} - kt$ | $\ln C_A$ vs. $t$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 2 | Second order ($n=2$, equal $C_{A0}$) | $-r_A = kC_A^2$ | $\dfrac{1}{C_A} = \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}} + kt$ | $1/C_A$ vs. $t$ | $\text{L mol}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 2* | Second order ($n=2$, $C_{A0} \neq C_{B0}$) | $-r_A = kC_A C_B$ | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_B C_{A0}}{C_A C_{B0}}\right) = (C_{A0}-C_{B0})\,kt$ | See derivation below | $\text{L mol}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| $n$ | General $n$-th order ($n\neq 1$) | $-r_A = kC_A^n$ | $C_A^{1-n} = C_{A0}^{1-n} + (n-1)kt$ | $C_A^{1-n}$ vs. $t$ | $\text{L}^{n-1}\text{mol}^{1-n}\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| — | Half-life (order $n\neq 1$) | — | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{2^{n-1}-1}{(n-1)k\,C_{A0}^{n-1}}$ | $\ln t_{1/2}$ vs. $\ln C_{A0}$ | — |
| — | Half-life (order $n = 1$) | — | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | — | — |
| — | Arrhenius Equation | — | $k = A\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$ | — |
| — | Linearized Arrhenius | — | $\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T}$ | $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$ | — |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Integrated Rate Law

**Starting differential equation:**

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = kC_A$$

**Step 1:** Separate variables by moving all $C_A$ terms to one side and $t$ terms to the other.

$$\frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -k\,dt$$

**Step 2:** Apply definite integration limits. At $t = 0$, $C_A = C_{A0}$. At general time $t$, $C_A = C_A$.

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} \frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -k\int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integrals. $\int dC_A/C_A = \ln C_A$ and $\int dt = t$.

$$\left[\ln C_A\right]_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = -kt$$

$$\ln C_A - \ln C_{A0} = -kt$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to the standard textbook form.

$$\boxed{\;\ln C_A = \ln C_{A0} - kt\;}$$

**Step 5 (Conversion logic):** This is a straight line when $\ln C_A$ is plotted against $t$. The slope equals $-k$ and the $y$-intercept equals $\ln C_{A0}$. This linear form is the basis for testing whether experimental data follows first-order kinetics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Second-Order Integrated Rate Law (Equal Initial Concentrations)

**Starting differential equation:**

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = kC_A^2$$

**Step 1:** Separate variables.

$$\frac{dC_A}{C_A^2} = -k\,dt$$

**Step 2:** Apply the same limits as before.

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} \frac{dC_A}{C_A^2} = -k\int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate. $\int dC_A/C_A^2 = -1/C_A$.

$$\left[-\frac{1}{C_A}\right]_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = -kt$$

$$-\frac{1}{C_A} + \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = -kt$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange.

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{C_A} = \frac{1}{C_{A0}} + kt\;}$$

**Step 5 (Conversion logic):** A plot of $1/C_A$ versus $t$ yields a straight line through the origin region with intercept $1/C_{A0}$ and slope $k$. This is the diagnostic test for second-order kinetics when $C_{A0} = C_{B0}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Second-Order Kinetics with $C_{A0} \neq C_{B0}$ (e.g., Saponification)

For $A + B \rightarrow \text{Products}$, with $-r_A = kC_A C_B$:

**Step 1:** Define the amount reacted. Let $x$ = moles/L of A that have reacted at time $t$.

$$C_A = C_{A0} - x \qquad C_B = C_{B0} - x$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into rate law. Since $-r_A = dx/dt$:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = k(C_{A0}-x)(C_{B0}-x)$$

**Step 3:** Partial fraction decomposition:

$$\frac{1}{(C_{A0}-x)(C_{B0}-x)} = \frac{1}{C_{A0}-C_{B0}}\left[\frac{1}{C_{B0}-x} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}-x}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Integrate.

$$\frac{1}{C_{A0}-C_{B0}}\int_{0}^{x}\!\left[\frac{1}{C_{B0}-x}-\frac{1}{C_{A0}-x}\right]dx = k\int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate and simplify:

$$\frac{1}{C_{A0}-C_{B0}}\left[-\ln(C_{B0}-x) + \ln(C_{A0}-x)\right]_{0}^{x} = kt$$

**Step 6:** Substitute limits and back-substitute $C_A, C_B$:

$$\frac{1}{C_{A0}-C_{B0}}\left[\ln\frac{C_A}{C_{B0}} - \ln\frac{C_{B0}}{C_{A0}}\right]\cdot (-1)\;\longrightarrow\;\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{C_B\,C_{A0}}{C_A\,C_{B0}}\right) = (C_{A0}-C_{B0})\,k\,t\;}$$

**Conversion logic:** Plot the left-hand side vs. $t$. A straight line through the origin with slope $(C_{A0}-C_{B0})k$ confirms second-order overall, first-order in each reactant.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: General $n$-th Order Integrated Law ($n \neq 1$)

Starting from $-dC_A/dt = k C_A^n$:

$$C_A^{-n}\,dC_A = -k\,dt$$

Integrate:

$$\frac{C_A^{1-n}}{1-n}\bigg|_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = -kt$$

$$\frac{C_A^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n}}{1-n} = -kt$$

Multiply both sides by $-1$:

$$\boxed{\;C_A^{1-n} = C_{A0}^{1-n} + (n-1)\,k\,t\;}$$

**Diagnostic use:** Plot $C_A^{1-n}$ vs. $t$ for trial values of $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, ...$. The $n$ that produces the most linear plot is the correct order.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Half-Life for $n$-th Order Reaction

**Step 1:** Set $C_A = C_{A0}/2$ in the general integrated form.

$$\left(\frac{C_{A0}}{2}\right)^{1-n} = C_{A0}^{1-n} + (n-1)\,k\,t_{1/2}$$

**Step 2:** Expand left side:

$$C_{A0}^{1-n}\cdot 2^{n-1} = C_{A0}^{1-n} + (n-1)k\,t_{1/2}$$

**Step 3:** Subtract $C_{A0}^{1-n}$:

$$C_{A0}^{1-n}\left(2^{n-1} - 1\right) = (n-1)k\,t_{1/2}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $t_{1/2}$:

$$\boxed{\;t_{1/2} = \frac{2^{n-1} - 1}{(n-1)\,k\,C_{A0}^{\,n-1}}\;}$$

**Conversion logic:** Take natural log:

$$\ln t_{1/2} = \ln\!\left(\frac{2^{n-1}-1}{(n-1)k}\right) + (1-n)\ln C_{A0}$$

Plot $\ln t_{1/2}$ vs. $\ln C_{A0}$. Slope $= 1 - n$, hence $n = 1 - \text{slope}$.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Arrhenius Equation and Activation Energy

**Step 1:** Start with the Arrhenius empirical equation.

$$k = A\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Apply natural logarithm:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize this as the linear form $Y = mX + b$ where $Y = \ln k$, $X = 1/T$, $m = -E_a/R$, $b = \ln A$.

**Step 4:** From a slope measured at two temperatures:

$$E_a = R\cdot\frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

**Step 5 (Engineering interpretation):** The pre-exponential factor $A$ represents the **frequency of molecular collisions with the correct orientation**, and $E_a$ is the **minimum energy barrier** reactants must overcome to form products.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Batch Reactor Setup Used in Module 2

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a typical batch reactor rig used for kinetic studies. Each block represents a physical or functional component, and arrows show the flow of material, energy, and information.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactant Solution A in Jacketed Glass Reactor] --> B[Thermostatic Water Bath]
    C[Reactant Solution B in Separate Vessel] --> A
    B --> A
    A --> D[Mechanical Stirrer with RPM Controller]
    A --> E[Sampling Port with Pipette]
    E --> F[Quench Bath Ice Cold Water]
    F --> G[Analytical Instrument Titration or Spectrophotometer]
    G --> H[Concentration vs Time Data Table]
    H --> I{Order Determination Method}
    I --> J[Integral Method]
    I --> K[Differential Method]
    I --> L[Half Life Method]
    J --> M[Calculate k at Each Temperature]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Arrhenius Plot ln k vs 1 over T]
    N --> O[Slope gives Ea over R]
    O --> P[Final Report k Ea and Reaction Order]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **double-arrow** between the thermostatic bath and reactor indicates heat exchange (the bath circulates water at constant $T$ through the jacket).
- The **sampling port** is the heart of kinetics work — its design (whether it allows quench-in-place or requires manual transfer) affects experimental accuracy.
- The **decision block** $I$ represents the three classical analysis paths converging on a single rate constant $k$ per temperature.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Kinetic Data Reduction Workflow

This block diagram captures the logical workflow a student must follow during post-experiment data analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RawData[Raw Experimental Data]
        R1[Time t in seconds]
        R2[Concentration CA in mol per L]
        R3[Temperature T in Kelvin]
    end

    subgraph Analysis[Analysis Stage]
        S1[Trial Fit Zero Order: CA vs t]
        S2[Trial Fit First Order: ln CA vs t]
        S3[Trial Fit Second Order: 1 over CA vs t]
        S4[Select Best Linear Fit R squared closest to 1]
        S5[Determine k from Slope]
        S6[Repeat at T2 and T3]
        S7[Plot ln k vs 1 over T]
    end

    subgraph Output[Final Outputs]
        F1[Reaction Order n]
        F2[Rate Constant k]
        F3[Activation Energy Ea]
        F4[Pre exponential Factor A]
    end

    R1 --> S1
    R1 --> S2
    R1 --> S3
    R2 --> S1
    R2 --> S2
    R2 --> S3
    S1 --> S4
    S2 --> S4
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    R3 --> S6
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> F3
    S7 --> F4
    S5 --> F2
    S4 --> F1
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Decision Tree for Order Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Have CA vs t Data] --> Q1{Is CA vs t linear?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Z[Zero Order: k from negative slope]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is ln CA vs t linear?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> O1[First Order: k from negative slope]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is 1 over CA vs t linear?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> O2[Second Order: k from slope]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4[Try half life method or differential method]
    Q4 --> O3[General Order n from log log plot]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Tip from the Bench:** In the KTU lab, students often observe that real data may not be perfectly linear due to heat-loss, evaporation, or sampling errors. Always report the **regression coefficient $R^2$** for each trial fit and choose the highest one as your reported order.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls Specific to Kinetics Problems**
> - **Units Trap:** Many students write $k$ without units. KTU examiners specifically check units; $-1$ mark per missing unit is common.
> - **Order ≠ Stoichiometry:** Writing "second order because two molecules react" is a guaranteed zero. Order is **experimental**.
> - **Plot Axes Confusion:** Examiners expect $Y$-axis label first, $X$-axis second (e.g., $\ln C_A$ on $Y$ vs. $t$ on $X$). Reversing earns a $-0.5$ mark.
> - **Sign of Slope:** For first order, slope $= -k$, so students must explicitly state $k = -\text{slope}$.
> - **Arrhenius Sign:** Slope is $-E_a/R$, so $E_a = -\text{slope} \times R$. A negative $E_a$ in your answer means you forgot the minus sign.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the order of a reaction. How is it determined experimentally?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **order of a reaction** with respect to a reactant is the **power to which its concentration is raised** in the experimentally observed rate law. The **overall order** is the sum of these individual orders.

For $A + B \rightarrow \text{Products}$, if $-r_A = k C_A^{\alpha} C_B^{\beta}$, then order with respect to $A$ is $\alpha$, with respect to $B$ is $\beta$, and overall order is $n = \alpha + \beta$.

It is **determined experimentally** using one of three classical methods:
- **Integral method:** Test fit of data against integrated forms of various orders.
- **Differential method:** Plot $\ln(-r_A)$ vs. $\ln C_A$ to find slope = $n$.
- **Half-life method:** Plot $\ln t_{1/2}$ vs. $\ln C_{A0}$; slope $= 1 - n$.

**(1 mark)** for definition; **(1 mark)** for overall order expression; **(1 mark)** for any one valid experimental method.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between rate constant and reaction rate. State the effect of temperature on the rate constant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Reaction Rate ($-r_A$)** | **Rate Constant ($k$)** |
|:-----------|:---------------------------|:------------------------|
| Definition | Rate at which reactant concentration decreases per unit time | Proportionality constant linking rate to concentration in the rate law |
| Dependence on $C_A$ | Yes | No |
| Dependence on $T$ | Yes (indirectly, via $k$) | Yes (strongly, via Arrhenius) |
| Units | $\text{mol L}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$ | Depends on order |

**Effect of temperature on $k$:** As temperature rises, $k$ **increases exponentially** according to the Arrhenius equation $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$. A typical 10 °C rise in temperature can double or triple the rate constant.

**(1.5 marks)** for the distinction table; **(1.5 marks)** for the Arrhenius-based effect of $T$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Derive the integrated rate expression for a **second-order reaction** $2A \rightarrow \text{Products}$ where the initial concentrations of A and any other reactant are equal. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** The saponification of ethyl acetate by NaOH is carried out in a batch reactor at 35 °C with initial concentrations $C_{A0} = C_{B0} = 0.05$ mol/L. The following data is obtained:

| Time (min) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 20 | 30 | 50 |
|:----------:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:--:|:--:|
| $C_A$ (mol/L) | 0.050 | 0.0335 | 0.0250 | 0.0167 | 0.0125 | 0.0083 |

Determine the **rate constant $k$** at 35 °C and the **half-life $t_{1/2}$**. The reaction is overall second-order. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** Write the differential rate law:

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = kC_A^2$$

**Step 2:** Separate variables.

$$\frac{dC_A}{C_A^2} = -k\,dt$$

**Step 3:** Integrate with limits $C_{A0} \to C_A$ over $0 \to t$:

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} C_A^{-2}\,dC_A = -k\int_{0}^{t}dt$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integral on the left using $\int x^{-2} dx = -x^{-1}$:

$$\left[-\frac{1}{C_A}\right]_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = -kt$$

**Step 5:** Expand the limits.

$$-\frac{1}{C_A} + \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = -kt$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to obtain the standard integrated form.

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{C_A} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = kt\;}$$

**Diagnostic check:** A plot of $1/C_A$ vs. $t$ yields a straight line with slope $k$ and intercept $1/C_{A0}$.

**Mark Allocation:** 
- Correct differential form **(1 mark)**
- Correct separation and integration setup **(2 marks)**
- Correct evaluation of integral **(2 marks)**
- Final boxed expression with proper diagnostic note **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1:** Calculate $1/C_A$ for each data point.

| $t$ (min) | $C_A$ (mol/L) | $1/C_A$ (L/mol) |
|:---------:|:-------------:|:---------------:|
| 0 | 0.0500 | 20.00 |
| 5 | 0.0335 | 29.85 |
| 10 | 0.0250 | 40.00 |
| 20 | 0.0167 | 59.88 |
| 30 | 0.0125 | 80.00 |
| 50 | 0.0083 | 120.48 |

**Step 2:** Perform linear regression. Slope $= k$ (units L mol⁻¹ min⁻¹).

Using least-squares:

$$\text{slope} = \frac{N\sum t\cdot(1/C_A) - \sum t \sum(1/C_A)}{N\sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2}$$

Computing the sums:
- $N = 6$
- $\sum t = 0 + 5 + 10 + 20 + 30 + 50 = 115$
- $\sum(1/C_A) = 20 + 29.85 + 40 + 59.88 + 80 + 120.48 = 350.21$
- $\sum t^2 = 0 + 25 + 100 + 400 + 900 + 2500 = 3925$
- $\sum t \cdot (1/C_A) = 0 + 149.25 + 400 + 1197.6 + 2400 + 6024 = 10170.85$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the slope formula.

$$k = \frac{6 \times 10170.85 - 115 \times 350.21}{6 \times 3925 - 115^2}$$

$$k = \frac{61025.1 - 40274.15}{23550 - 13225} = \frac{20750.95}{10325}$$

$$\boxed{\;k \approx 2.010 \;\text{L mol}^{-1}\text{min}^{-1}\;}$$

**Step 4:** Compute half-life using the second-order formula.

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{1}{k\,C_{A0}}$$

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{1}{2.010 \times 0.050} = \frac{1}{0.1005}$$

$$\boxed{\;t_{1/2} \approx 9.95 \;\text{min} \approx 10 \;\text{min}\;}$$

**Mark Allocation:**
- Correct table of $1/C_A$ values **(1 mark)**
- Setting up linear regression or correctly identifying two-point slope **(2 marks)**
- Final value of $k$ with units **(2 marks)**
- Correct half-life formula and final value **(2 marks)**

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** With the help of the Arrhenius equation, derive a **linearized form** suitable for plotting. Explain how the **activation energy $E_a$** is obtained from experimental data. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** The rate constant for a first-order liquid-phase reaction was measured at three temperatures:

| Temperature $T$ (K) | Rate constant $k$ (s⁻¹) |
|:-------------------:|:-----------------------:|
| 303 | $1.50 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 313 | $4.20 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 323 | $1.10 \times 10^{-2}$ |

Calculate the **activation energy $E_a$** (in kJ/mol) using the slope of $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** State the Arrhenius equation.

$$k = A\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Apply natural logarithm to both sides.

$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize this is in the linear form $Y = mX + c$:

- $Y = \ln k$
- $X = 1/T$
- Slope $m = -E_a/R$
- Intercept $c = \ln A$

**Step 4:** Therefore, a plot of $\ln k$ on the $Y$-axis versus $1/T$ on the $X$-axis yields a straight line. The slope is $-E_a/R$, and the intercept gives $\ln A$.

**Step 5 (Experimental extraction):** From two data points at $T_1, k_1$ and $T_2, k_2$:

$$\text{slope} = \frac{\ln k_2 - \ln k_1}{\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}} = -\frac{E_a}{R}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;E_a = -R\cdot\frac{\ln k_2 - \ln k_1}{\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}} = \frac{R\,\ln(k_2/k_1)}{\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}}\;}$$

**Mark Allocation:**
- Correct Arrhenius statement **(1 mark)**
- Taking ln and isolating linear form **(2 marks)**
- Identifying slope and intercept **(2 marks)**
- Final boxed expression for $E_a$ from data **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1:** Compute $1/T$ and $\ln k$ for each row.

| $T$ (K) | $1/T$ (K⁻¹) | $k$ (s⁻¹) | $\ln k$ |
|:-------:|:-----------:|:---------:|:-------:|
| 303 | $3.300 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1.50 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-6.502$ |
| 313 | $3.195 \times 10^{-3}$ | $4.20 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-5.473$ |
| 323 | $3.096 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1.10 \times 10^{-2}$ | $-4.510$ |

**Step 2:** Apply least-squares for slope.

Let $x = 1/T$ and $y = \ln k$. With $N = 3$:

- $\sum x = 3.300 + 3.195 + 3.096 = 9.591 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹
- $\sum y = -6.502 - 5.473 - 4.510 = -16.485$
- $\sum x^2 = (3.300^2 + 3.195^2 + 3.096^2)\times 10^{-6} = (10.89 + 10.21 + 9.585)\times 10^{-6} = 30.685 \times 10^{-6}$ K⁻²
- $\sum xy = [3.300\times(-6.502) + 3.195\times(-5.473) + 3.096\times(-4.510)]\times 10^{-3} = [-21.457 - 17.486 - 13.963]\times 10^{-3} = -52.906 \times 10^{-3}$

**Step 3:** Apply the slope formula.

$$\text{slope} = \frac{N\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{N\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$$

$$\text{slope} = \frac{3\times(-52.906\times 10^{-3}) - (9.591\times 10^{-3})(-16.485)}{3\times(30.685\times 10^{-6}) - (9.591\times 10^{-3})^2}$$

**Numerator:**
$$3\times(-52.906\times 10^{-3}) = -158.718 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$- (9.591 \times 10^{-3})(-16.485) = +158.108 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$\text{Numerator} = -158.718 + 158.108 = -0.610 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Denominator:**
$$3 \times 30.685 \times 10^{-6} = 92.055 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$(9.591 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 91.987 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 0.068 \times 10^{-6} = 6.8 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\text{slope} = \frac{-0.610 \times 10^{-3}}{6.8 \times 10^{-8}} = -8970.6 \;\text{K}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate $E_a$.

$$E_a = -\text{slope}\times R = -(-8970.6)\times 8.314 = 8970.6 \times 8.314 \;\text{J/mol}$$

$$E_a = 74{,}581\;\text{J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\;E_a \approx 74.58 \;\text{kJ/mol}\;}$$

**Sanity check:** Most liquid-phase reactions have $E_a$ between 40–100 kJ/mol. The value of 74.58 kJ/mol is in the realistic range. ✓

**Mark Allocation:**
- Correct $\ln k$ and $1/T$ table **(1 mark)**
- Setting up least-squares formula **(2 marks)**
- Correct slope value **(2 marks)**
- Final $E_a$ in kJ/mol with units **(2 marks)**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2: Kinetics of Reactions**

- ☐ **Rate Law:** Always written as $-r_A = k C_A^{\alpha} C_B^{\beta} \cdots$. Order is **experimental**, not stoichiometric.
- ☐ **Overall Order:** $n = \alpha + \beta + \gamma + \cdots$ — a dimensionless number that can be **integer, fractional, or zero**.
- ☐ **Three Diagnostic Plots:** $C_A$ vs. $t$ → 0 order; $\ln C_A$ vs. $t$ → 1st order; $1/C_A$ vs. $t$ → 2nd order.
- ☐ **Slope Sign Convention:** 1st-order slope = $-k$, so $k = -\text{slope}$ — **never** report negative $k$.
- ☐ **Half-life Formulas:** $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$ for 1st order (independent of $C_{A0}$); $t_{1/2} = 1/(kC_{A0})$ for 2nd order with equal initial concentrations.
- ☐ **Arrhenius:** $\ln k = \ln A - (E_a/R)(1/T)$. Slope of $\ln k$ vs. $1/T$ = $-E_a/R$. Always report $E_a$ in **kJ/mol**.
- ☐ **R constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). Convert to kJ by dividing answer by 1000.
- ☐ **Sample Quenching:** Always quench samples immediately to "freeze" the reaction; otherwise your measured $C_A$ corresponds to a longer time than recorded.
- ☐ **Isothermal Assumption:** A batch kinetics experiment assumes **constant $T$**. Verify using the thermostatic bath controller reading at every sample.
- ☐ **Units Discipline:** $k$ has units of $\text{L}^{n-1}\text{mol}^{1-n}\text{s}^{-1}$. Always attach units to $k$ and $E_a$.
- ☐ **R² Reporting:** For each trial fit, compute the coefficient of determination $R^2$; the order with $R^2$ closest to 1 is the chosen order.
- ☐ **Stoichiometric vs. Kinetic Order:** They **coincide** only for elementary reactions; for complex reactions they differ — the experiment decides.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinetics of Homogeneous Chemical Reactions Using Simulation Software (MATLAB or SCILAB)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL507 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Kinetics of Homogeneous Chemical Reactions Using Simulation Software (MATLAB or SCILAB)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Chemical Reaction Engineering (CRE), a **homogeneous reaction** is a reaction in which all reactants and products exist in a single physical phase (gaseous, liquid, or solid) throughout the course of the reaction. The reaction kinetics — i.e., the mathematical relationship between the rate of reaction and the concentrations (or partial pressures) of the reacting species — can be described by a **rate law** of the form:

$$-r_A = k \cdot C_A^n$$

where $-r_A$ is the rate of disappearance of the limiting reactant A (in kmol·m⁻³·s⁻¹), $k$ is the **specific reaction rate constant**, $C_A$ is the concentration of A, and $n$ is the **order of reaction** with respect to A.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PCCHL507, Module Experiment 2**
> The objective of this experiment is to determine the **rate constant (k)** and the **reaction order (n)** of a homogeneous reaction by acquiring **simulated concentration–time data** from MATLAB or SCILAB and then fitting the data using integral, differential, or nonlinear regression methods.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition

Imagine a crowded classroom (the *reaction volume*) where students represent molecules of reactant A. Every time two students (or one student, depending on the order) bump into each other with enough energy, they "react" and leave the room (become product). The rate at which students leave depends on how crowded the room is — empty the room is slow, full room is fast. This crowd-density dependence is exactly the **concentration dependence ($C_A$)**, and the way the crowd empties follows a mathematical curve that we can record and fit.

In this experiment, instead of doing a wet-lab titration every few seconds, we use a **simulator** (MATLAB or SCILAB) to numerically integrate the governing ODE:

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k \cdot C_A^n$$

and generate a clean, noiseless dataset $(t_i, C_{A,i})$. The student then applies analytical methods to recover $k$ and $n$ — the two unknowns hidden in the curve.

### 1.3 Why Use Simulation Software?

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reaction Studied in KTU Labs:** Saponification of ethyl acetate with sodium hydroxide
> $$\text{CH}_3\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COONa} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$
> This is an isothermal, **second-order** homogeneous liquid-phase reaction with equal initial concentrations of both reactants.

Simulation is preferred because:
- **No chemical waste**, no toxicity, no cost of reagents.
- **Exactness**: The simulator generates ideal data; students learn the *mathematics of fitting* first, before tackling real noisy data.
- **Repeatability**: The same "experiment" can be re-run for different assumed $k$ and $n$ to study the effect.
- **Reproducibility of results** in the lab record, important for KTU internal evaluation.

**Standard physical parameters used in the simulation:**
- Initial concentration $C_{A0} = C_{B0} = \mathbf{0.05 \; kmol \cdot m^{-3}}$ (or 0.1 N)
- Temperature $T = \mathbf{298 \; K}$ (isothermal)
- Typical true value of $k = \mathbf{0.006 \; m^3 \cdot kmol^{-1} \cdot s^{-1}}$ (second-order)
- Simulation time span: **0 to 3600 s** with time-step $\Delta t = \mathbf{60 \; s}$

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Model for a Batch Reactor

For an isothermal constant-volume batch reactor (which is exactly what a beaker or test-tube represents in this experiment), the **mole balance** on reactant A reduces to a simple ODE:

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A \cdot V \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k \cdot C_A^n$$

Because volume $V$ is constant, $C_A = N_A / V$. This ODE is the **heart** of the entire experiment.

### 2.2 Integrated Rate Laws (The "Cheat Sheet")

The ODE above can be integrated analytically for integer orders. These are the **exact analytical solutions** that the simulator's numerical output must obey.

| Reaction Order ($n$) | Integrated Form (Linearized) | Slope = ? | Intercept = ? | Units of $k$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Zero** ($n = 0$) | $C_A = C_{A0} - k t$ | $-k$ | $C_{A0}$ | kmol·m⁻³·s⁻¹ |
| **First** ($n = 1$) | $\ln C_A = \ln C_{A0} - k t$ | $-k$ | $\ln C_{A0}$ | s⁻¹ |
| **Second (equal conc.)** ($n = 2$, $C_{A0} = C_{B0}$) | $\dfrac{1}{C_A} = \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}} + k t$ | $+k$ | $\dfrac{1}{C_{A0}}$ | m³·kmol⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| **Second (unequal conc.)** ($n = 2$, $C_{A0} \ne C_{B0}$) | $\ln \dfrac{C_B \, C_A}{C_{B0} \, C_A} \cdot \dfrac{C_{A0}}{C_B} \Big) = (C_{A0} - C_{B0}) k t$ | $(C_{A0}-C_{B0})k$ | 0 | m³·kmol⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| **n-th order** (general) | $\dfrac{1}{C_A^{n-1}} - \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}^{n-1}} = (n-1) k t$ | $(n-1)k$ | $C_{A0}^{1-n}$ | (m³)^(n-1)·(kmol)^(1-n)·s⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **slope of the linearized plot** directly gives the rate constant $k$. The **linearity** of the plot (highest $R^2$) identifies the correct reaction order. This is the **Integral Method of Analysis**.

### 2.3 The Three Analysis Methods (Mandatory in KTU Record)

**(a) Integral Method** — Try each integer order, plot the appropriate linearized form, and pick the order giving the best straight line ($R^2 \to 1$).

**(b) Differential Method** — Plot $-dC_A/dt$ vs $C_A$ on log-log paper. The slope of $\log(-r_A)$ vs $\log C_A$ gives $n$ directly, and the intercept gives $\log k$.

$$\log(-r_A) = \log k + n \log C_A$$

**(c) Nonlinear Least-Squares Regression** (preferred when using MATLAB/SCILAB) — Minimize the sum of squared residuals between simulated data and the model:

$$\min_{k,\,n} \; \Phi(k,n) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ C_{A,i}^{\text{sim}} - C_{A,i}^{\text{model}}(k,n,t_i) \right]^2$$

The `lsqnonlin` (MATLAB) or `lsqrsolve` / `datafit` (SCILAB) optimizers solve this.

### 2.4 Role of MATLAB / SCILAB in the Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> SCILAB is **open-source** and freely downloadable — it is the **default choice** in most KTU-affiliated colleges. MATLAB is acceptable when a campus license is available. The numerical syntax is virtually identical for the operations used here.

| Function | MATLAB | SCILAB |
|---|---|---|
| ODE solver | `ode45` | `ode` |
| Nonlinear regression | `lsqnonlin` | `lsqrsolve`, `datafit` |
| Linear regression | `polyfit` | `polyfit` |
| Plot | `plot(x,y)` | `plot(x,y)` |
| Curve fit toolbox GUI | `cftool` | `Datafit` (ATOMS module) |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

The fitted values of $k$ and $n$ are the **kinetic fingerprint** of the reaction. They directly enter the **design equations** for industrial reactors:

- **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR):** $\; \tau = \displaystyle\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_{Af}} \dfrac{dC_A}{-r_A}$
- **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR):** $\; \tau = \dfrac{C_{A0} - C_{Af}}{-r_{Af}}$

Without correct $k$ and $n$, no industrial reactor can be sized correctly.
