## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Integrated Rate Law for a Second-Order Reaction with Equal Initial Concentrations

We begin from the mole balance for an isothermal constant-volume batch reactor:

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k \cdot C_A^2$$

**Step 1 — Separate the variables.**
Move all $C_A$ terms to the left-hand side and $t$ to the right-hand side:

$$\frac{dC_A}{C_A^2} = -k \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides** between the limits $t = 0$ (where $C_A = C_{A0}$) and $t = t$ (where $C_A = C_A$).

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} C_A^{-2} \, dC_A = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the left-hand integral.**

$$\int C_A^{-2} \, dC_A = -C_A^{-1}$$

Applying the limits:

$$\left[ -C_A^{-1} \right]_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = -\frac{1}{C_A} + \frac{1}{C_{A0}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the right-hand integral.**

$$-k [t]_0^t = -k t$$

**Step 5 — Equate and simplify.**

$$-\frac{1}{C_A} + \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = -k t$$

Multiply by $-1$:

$$\frac{1}{C_A} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = k t$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{C_A} = \frac{1}{C_{A0}} + k t\;}$$

This is the **canonical linearized form** used to identify and quantify a second-order reaction.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — General n-th Order Integrated Rate Law

Starting ODE:

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k \cdot C_A^n$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables.**

$$C_A^{-n} \, dC_A = -k \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Integrate** (assuming $n \neq 1$).

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} C_A^{-n} \, dC_A = -k \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 3 — Apply the power rule** $\int x^m \, dx = \dfrac{x^{m+1}}{m+1}$ with $m = -n$:

$$\left[ \frac{C_A^{-n+1}}{-n+1} \right]_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = -k t$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$\frac{C_A^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n}}{1-n} = -k t$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $(1-n)$ and rearrange.**

$$\boxed{\;C_A^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n} = (n-1) k t\;}$$

For $n = 2$, this collapses to $\dfrac{1}{C_A} - \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}} = k t$, confirming Derivation 1.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Differential Method (Log-Log Slope Gives $n$)

**Step 1 — Take the rate equation.**

$$-r_A = k C_A^n$$

**Step 2 — Take the base-10 logarithm of both sides.**

$$\log(-r_A) = \log(k \, C_A^n) = \log k + n \log C_A$$

**Step 3 — Identify the linear form** $Y = mX + b$.

$$Y = \log(-r_A), \quad X = \log C_A, \quad m = n, \quad b = \log k$$

**Step 4 — Slope of the log-log plot gives $n$, intercept gives $k$.**

$$\boxed{\;n = \dfrac{d \log(-r_A)}{d \log C_A}, \qquad k = 10^{b}\;}$$

In practice, students approximate $-r_A$ at each data point using a **finite-difference derivative**:

$$-r_A \Big|_{t_i} \approx -\frac{C_{A,i+1} - C_{A,i-1}}{t_{i+1} - t_{i-1}}$$

This is the **central-difference** formula with truncation error $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t^2)$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Sample KTU-Style Problem)

> **Given:** Simulated batch data for the saponification of ethyl acetate at 298 K, with $C_{A0} = 0.05$ kmol/m³.
>
> | $t$ (s) | 0 | 300 | 600 | 900 | 1200 | 1800 | 2400 | 3000 | 3600 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | $C_A$ (kmol/m³) | 0.0500 | 0.0385 | 0.0313 | 0.0263 | 0.0227 | 0.0179 | 0.0149 | 0.0128 | 0.0112 |

**(a) Test for first order.** Compute $\ln C_A$:

| $t$ | $C_A$ | $\ln C_A$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0500 | $-3.000$ |
| 600 | 0.0313 | $-3.464$ |
| 1200 | 0.0227 | $-3.785$ |
| 1800 | 0.0179 | $-4.022$ |
| 2400 | 0.0149 | $-4.207$ |
| 3000 | 0.0128 | $-4.359$ |
| 3600 | 0.0112 | $-4.492$ |

Differences are **not constant**, so first order is rejected.

**(b) Test for second order** (equal initial concentrations). Compute $1/C_A$:

| $t$ | $C_A$ | $1/C_A$ (m³/kmol) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0500 | 20.00 |
| 600 | 0.0313 | 31.95 |
| 1200 | 0.0227 | 44.05 |
| 1800 | 0.0179 | 55.87 |
| 2400 | 0.0149 | 67.11 |
| 3000 | 0.0128 | 78.13 |
| 3600 | 0.0112 | 89.29 |

**Step 1 — Compute differences $\Delta(1/C_A)$ between successive rows** separated by $\Delta t = 600$ s:

| Interval | $\Delta(1/C_A)$ |
|---|---|
| 0 → 600 | 11.95 |
| 600 → 1200 | 12.10 |
| 1200 → 1800 | 11.82 |
| 1800 → 2400 | 11.24 |
| 2400 → 3000 | 11.02 |
| 3000 → 3600 | 11.16 |

Differences are **approximately constant** $\approx 11.55$. This confirms **second order**.

**Step 2 — Compute slope** using linear regression of $1/C_A$ vs $t$:

$$\text{slope} = \frac{N\sum t_i y_i - \sum t_i \sum y_i}{N \sum t_i^2 - (\sum t_i)^2}$$

Using the seven data points (with $y = 1/C_A$):

$$\sum t_i = 12600, \quad \sum y_i = 386.40, \quad \sum t_i y_i = 825\,312, \quad \sum t_i^2 = 32\,940\,000, \quad N = 7$$

$$\text{slope} = \frac{7(825\,312) - (12600)(386.40)}{7(32\,940\,000) - (12600)^2}$$

$$= \frac{5\,777\,184 - 4\,868\,640}{230\,580\,000 - 158\,760\,000} = \frac{908\,544}{71\,820\,000}$$

$$\boxed{\;k \approx 0.01265 \; \frac{m^3}{kmol \cdot s}\;}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.** The fitted $k$ is close to the true simulator value $k_{\text{true}} = 0.0125$ m³/(kmol·s). The order is unambiguously $n = 2$ from the linearity of $1/C_A$ vs $t$.

---

### 3.5 SCILAB / MATLAB Code Snippet (Authorized — This Is the Lab Topic)

```scilab
// SCILAB script: simulate and fit saponification kinetics
// Step 1 — Define the ODE
function dCdt = kinetics(t, C, k, n)
    dCdt = -k * C^n;
endfunction

// Step 2 — "True" parameters
k_true = 0.0125;   // m3/(kmol.s)
n_true = 2;
CA0    = 0.05;     // kmol/m3
t      = 0:60:3600;

// Step 3 — Simulate
CA_sim = ode(CA0, t, list(kinetics, k_true, n_true));

// Step 4 — Fit using integral (second-order) method
y = 1 ./ CA_sim;
[p, S] = polyfit(t, y, 1);     // p(1) = slope = k, p(2) = intercept = 1/CA0
k_fit  = p(1);
CA0_fit = 1 / p(2);

printf("Fitted k = %.5f m3/(kmol.s)\n", k_fit);
printf("Fitted CA0 = %.5f kmol/m3\n", CA0_fit);
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the SCILAB output** is the actual assessment: students must show the plot of $1/C_A$ vs $t$ along with the slope/intercept values in the lab record.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Reaction Order

| Test Performed | Linearized Form | Successful If… | Order Found | Numerical Slope |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_A$ vs $t$ linear? | $C_A = C_{A0} - k t$ | $R^2 \approx 1$ | $n = 0$ | $-k$ |
| $\ln C_A$ vs $t$ linear? | $\ln C_A = \ln C_{A0} - k t$ | $R^2 \approx 1$ | $n = 1$ | $-k$ |
| $1/C_A$ vs $t$ linear? | $1/C_A = 1/C_{A0} + k t$ | $R^2 \approx 1$ | $n = 2$ | $+k$ |
| $1/C_A^2$ vs $t$ linear? | $1/C_A^2 = 1/C_{A0}^2 + 2 k t$ | $R^2 \approx 1$ | $n = 3$ | $+2k$ |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
What is meant by the **order of a homogeneous reaction**? How is it determined using the **method of initial rates**?

> **Model Answer:**
> The order of a homogeneous reaction is the exponent $n$ in the power-law rate expression $-r_A = k C_A^n$. It indicates the dependence of the reaction rate on the concentration of A. In the method of initial rates, several batch experiments are performed (or simulated) with different initial concentrations $C_{A0,i}$ but identical temperature, and the initial rate $-r_{A0,i}$ is measured from the very early slope of the $C_A$ vs $t$ curve. Plotting $\log(-r_{A0})$ against $\log(C_{A0})$ gives a straight line of slope $n$ and intercept $\log k$, from which both $k$ and $n$ are extracted. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for procedure, 1 for graphical interpretation.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
Distinguish between the **integral method** and the **differential method** of kinetic analysis for a homogeneous reaction.

> **Model Answer:**
> The **integral method** assumes a trial order $n$, integrates the batch-reactor ODE $dC_A/dt = -kC_A^n$ analytically to obtain a linearized form (e.g., $\ln C_A$ vs $t$ for $n = 1$), and checks whether the data fit a straight line. The order with the best $R^2$ is selected, and the slope gives $k$.
> The **differential method** does *not* assume any order. The rate $-r_A$ is first estimated from the slope $-dC_A/dt$ of the experimental $C_A$ vs $t$ curve, and then $\log(-r_A)$ is plotted against $\log C_A$. The slope of this log-log plot directly equals the order $n$, and the intercept gives $k$. The differential method is more general but is sensitive to numerical differentiation noise. *(3 marks — 1.5 marks each for the two methods.)*

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

#### Solution to Question A(a) — Derivation

**Step 1.** Mole balance for constant-volume batch reactor:
$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k C_A^2$$

**Step 2.** Separate variables: $\dfrac{dC_A}{C_A^2} = -k \, dt$

**Step 3.** Integrate from $t = 0,\,C_A = C_{A0}$ to $t = t,\,C_A = C_A$:
$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} C_A^{-2}\,dC_A = -k\int_0^t dt$$

**Step 4.** Left side: $\left[ -\dfrac{1}{C_A} \right]_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = -\dfrac{1}{C_A} + \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}}$

**Step 5.** Right side: $-k t$

**Step 6.** Equate and simplify:
$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{1}{C_A} - \dfrac{1}{C_{A0}} = k t\;}$$

This is the linearized form used to verify second-order behaviour. **(7 marks — 1 per step plus 1 for final boxed result.)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(b) — Integral Method Identification

**Step 1.** Compute $1/C_A$ for each data point:

| $t$ (s) | $C_A$ | $1/C_A$ (m³/kmol) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0400 | 25.00 |
| 200 | 0.0308 | 32.47 |
| 400 | 0.0250 | 40.00 |
| 600 | 0.0211 | 47.39 |
| 800 | 0.0182 | 54.95 |
| 1000 | 0.0161 | 62.11 |
| 1200 | 0.0144 | 69.44 |

**Step 2.** Compute successive differences of $1/C_A$ (separated by $\Delta t = 200$ s):

| Interval | $\Delta(1/C_A)$ |
|---|---|
| 0 → 200 | 7.47 |
| 200 → 400 | 7.53 |
| 400 → 600 | 7.39 |
| 600 → 800 | 7.56 |
| 800 → 1000 | 7.16 |
| 1000 → 1200 | 7.33 |

Differences are nearly constant ⇒ **second order is confirmed**.

**Step 3.** Linear regression on $(t,\,1/C_A)$ using
$$\text{slope} = \frac{N\sum t_i y_i - \sum t_i \sum y_i}{N\sum t_i^2 - (\sum t_i)^2}$$

With $N = 7$, $\sum t_i = 4200$, $\sum y_i = 331.36$, $\sum t_i y_i = 248\,988$, $\sum t_i^2 = 3\,640\,000$:

$$\text{slope} = \frac{7(248\,988) - (4200)(331.36)}{7(3\,640\,000) - (4200)^2} = \frac{1\,742\,916 - 1\,391\,712}{25\,480\,000 - 17\,640\,000}$$

$$= \frac{351\,204}{7\,840\,000} \approx 0.0448$$

$$\boxed{\;k \approx 0.0448 \;\frac{m^3}{kmol \cdot s}, \quad n = 2\;}$$

**(7 marks — 2 for table, 2 for order confirmation, 2 for slope computation, 1 for final answer.)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(a) — Software Procedure Block Diagram

**Step 1.** Define the stoichiometry and write the rate law $-r_A = k C_A^n$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2.** Write the governing batch-reactor ODE $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = -k C_A^n$ in a function file (`function dCdt = kinetics(t,C,k,n); dCdt = -k*C^n; end`). *(1 mark)*

**Step 3.** Specify simulation inputs: $C_{A0} = 0.05$ kmol/m³, $k = 0.0125$, $n = 2$, $t = 0{:}60{:}3600$ s. *(1 mark)*

**Step 4.** Call the ODE solver: `CA_sim = ode(CA0, t, list(kinetics, k, n));` in SCILAB or `[t,CA] = ode45(@(t,C) -k*C^n, tspan, CA0);` in MATLAB. *(1 mark)*

**Step 5.** Compute linearized quantities: $y_0 = C_A$, $y_1 = \log C_A$, $y_2 = 1/C_A$, $y_3 = 1/C_A^2$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 6.** Apply `polyfit(t, y_i, 1)` for each $i$ and record the slope and $R^2$. The order with $R^2$ closest to 1 is the correct order. *(1 mark)*

**Step 7.** Extract $k$ from the slope of the correct plot and report the final values along with a plot (using `plot(t, 1./CA_sim, 'o')`). *(1 mark)*

**(7 marks distributed across the seven steps above.)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(b) — Differential Method

**Step 1.** Compute $-r_A$ at each interior point using central differences with $\Delta t = 600$ s:

$$-r_A(t_i) \approx \frac{C_{A,i-1} - C_{A,i+1}}{2 \Delta t}$$

| $t$ (s) | $C_A$ (kmol/m³) | $-r_A \times 10^5$ (kmol·m⁻³·s⁻¹) | $C_A$ (avg) | $\log C_A$ | $\log(-r_A)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0500 | — | — | — | — |
| 600 | 0.0385 | $\frac{0.0500 - 0.0313}{1200} = 1.558$ | 0.0385 | $-1.414$ | $-4.807$ |
| 1200 | 0.0313 | $\frac{0.0385 - 0.0263}{1200} = 1.017$ | 0.0313 | $-1.504$ | $-4.993$ |
| 1800 | 0.0263 | $\frac{0.0313 - 0.0227}{1200} = 0.717$ | 0.0263 | $-1.580$ | $-5.145$ |
| 2400 | 0.0227 | $\frac{0.0263 - 0.0199}{1200} = 0.533$ | 0.0227 | $-1.644$ | $-5.273$ |
| 3000 | 0.0199 | $\frac{0.0227 - 0.0179}{1200} = 0.400$ | 0.0199 | $-1.701$ | $-5.398$ |
| 3600 | 0.0179 | — | — | — | — |

**Step 2.** Linear regression of $\log(-r_A)$ vs $\log C_A$:

$$n = \frac{N\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$$

With $N = 5$, $\sum x_i = -7.843$, $\sum y_i = -25.616$, $\sum x_i y_i = 40.413$, $\sum x_i^2 = 12.402$:

$$n = \frac{5(40.413) - (-7.843)(-25.616)}{5(12.402) - (-7.843)^2} = \frac{202.065 - 200.945}{62.010 - 61.512} = \frac{1.120}{0.498}$$

$$\boxed{\;n \approx 2.25 \;\Rightarrow\; n \approx 2\;}$$

**Step 3.** Intercept $b = \dfrac{\sum y_i - n \sum x_i}{N} = \dfrac{-25.616 - (2.25)(-7.843)}{5} = \dfrac{-25.616 + 17.647}{5} = -1.594$

$$k = 10^{b} = 10^{-1.594} = 0.0255 \;\text{m}^3\cdot\text{kmol}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\;n \approx 2, \quad k \approx 0.025 \; \frac{m^3}{kmol \cdot s}\;}$$

**(7 marks — 2 for derivative table, 3 for regression to get $n$, 2 for $k$.)**

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this experiment:**
> 1. **Units of $k$**: Students frequently write $k$ without units. Examiner deducts 1 mark. Always write **units** explicitly — they vary with order ($s^{-1}$, $m^3 \cdot kmol^{-1} \cdot s^{-1}$, etc.).
> 2. **Mixing integral and differential plots**: Do not compute $\log(-r_A)$ and then plot it against $t$ — this is meaningless. It must be plotted against $\log C_A$.
> 3. **Forgetting $C_{A0} = C_{B0}$ assumption**: For unequal initial concentrations, the integrated form is different. State the assumption explicitly.
> 4. **Rounding prematurely**: Carry at least 4 significant digits until the final answer.
> 5. **Missing the SCILAB/MATLAB plot in the lab record**: The viva voce almost always asks the student to show the plot. Carry a printout or screenshot in the record.
> 6. **Confusing $r_A$ (rate of formation) with $-r_A$ (rate of disappearance)**: Always state the sign convention clearly.
> 7. **Not reporting $R^2$**: Mention the coefficient of determination when comparing candidate orders; it is the deciding evidence for the "best" order.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Homogeneous reaction kinetics** is governed by $-r_A = k C_A^n$ in a constant-volume batch reactor.
- The **mole balance** reduces to a single ODE: $dC_A/dt = -k C_A^n$.
- **Integral method**: Try integer orders $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$; plot the corresponding linearized form vs $t$; pick the most linear one (highest $R^2$); slope gives $k$.
- **Differential method**: Estimate $-r_A$ via finite differences; plot $\log(-r_A)$ vs $\log C_A$; slope = $n$, intercept = $\log k$.
- **Nonlinear regression** in SCILAB/MATLAB is the modern, robust alternative to graphical methods.
- **Standard KTU reaction**: Saponification of ethyl acetate with NaOH (second-order, $k \approx 0.0125$ m³/(kmol·s) at 298 K).
- **Units of $k$ depend on order**: Always write them explicitly.
- **SCILAB** is open-source and the default in most KTU-affiliated labs; MATLAB is accepted where licensed.
- The fitted $k$ and $n$ feed directly into **PFR** ($\tau = \int dC_A / -r_A$) and **CSTR** ($\tau = (C_{A0} - C_{Af}) / -r_{Af}$) design equations.
- Always include the **plot**, **slope**, **intercept**, **$R^2$**, **units**, and a one-line **conclusion** in the lab record.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of Ideal Reactors Using MATLAB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHL507 - CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Simulation of Ideal Reactors Using MATLAB ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Chemical Reaction Engineering (CRE) Lab (PCCHL507)** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, *Simulation of Ideal Reactors using MATLAB* refers to the computational emulation of three idealized reactor models — the **Batch Reactor (BR)**, **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)**, and **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** — by numerically solving their governing material balance equations (ordinary differential equations, or ODEs) on the MATLAB platform. This allows a chemical engineering student to predict **conversion ($X_A$)**, **concentration ($C_A$)**, **residence time ($\tau$)**, and **reactor volume ($V$)** without performing a physical wet-lab experiment. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCHL507):** > "Simulation of ideal reactors (Batch, CSTR, PFR) using MATLAB/Scilab for single and multiple reactions, isothermal conditions, and determination of conversion as a function of residence time or reactor volume." ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Kitchen" Analogy for Reactor Types:** > - **Batch Reactor** = A closed pressure cooker. You put all ingredients in, seal it, cook for a fixed time, and then open it. Nothing enters or leaves during cooking. > - **CSTR** = A large washing machine tub with continuous inflow of dirty water and outflow of clean water. The water inside is always perfectly mixed (like a giant cup of tea stirred by a tornado). > - **PFR** = A long, straight pipe (like a long straw) where fluid flows in one direction. Each droplet spends the same amount of time inside, like cars on a one-way highway lane. > > MATLAB acts as the **"virtual stopwatch and measuring cup"** that calculates how much chemical "cooks" (reacts) inside each vessel, saving the real-world cost of chemicals, glassware, and time. ### 1.3 Scope of the Lab Experiment The standard KTU Module Experiment 2 typically covers: 1. **Single reaction simulation** (e.g., $A \rightarrow B$, first-order kinetics) 2. **Multiple reaction simulation** (e.g., $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$ series, or $A \rightarrow B$, $A \rightarrow C$ parallel) 3. **Effect of temperature** via the Arrhenius equation (sometimes included in advanced variants) 4. **Comparison of reactor volumes** for a given conversion target **Key Standard Parameters:** - **Universal Gas Constant ($R$) = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** - **Standard Temperature ($T$) = 298 K** (unless otherwise specified) - **Liquid-phase assumption (constant density)** unless gas-phase kinetics are explicitly mentioned - **Isothermal condition**: $T$ is constant inside the reactor ### 1.4 Why MATLAB and Not Manual Calculation? Manual integration of design equations becomes impossible for complex kinetics (e.g., reversible, autocatalytic, or polymerization reactions). MATLAB's built-in **ODE solvers** (`ode45`, `ode23s`, `ode15s`) provide: - **Speed** — solutions in milliseconds. - **Accuracy** — adaptive step-size control. - **Visualization** — immediate plots of $C_A$ vs $\tau$, $X_A$ vs $V$, etc. - **Flexibility** — easily change $k$, $C_{A0}$, $F_{A0}$, or feed composition. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Governing Material Balance (Mole Balance) Framework Every ideal reactor model is derived from the **General Mole Balance Equation**: $$\text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}$$ For a single reactant $A$ undergoing reaction $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$, this becomes: $$F_{A0} - F_A + r_A \, V = \frac{dN_A}{dt}$$ Where: - $F_{A0}$ = molar flow rate of $A$ entering (mol/s) - $F_A$ = molar flow rate of $A$ leaving (mol/s) - $r_A$ = rate of disappearance of $A$ (mol/(m³·s)) — **negative** by convention - $V$ = reactor volume (m³) - $N_A$ = moles of $A$ inside the reactor ### 2.2 Reactor-Specific Forms of the Mole Balance > [!NOTE] > These three equations form the **TRINITY** of CRE design. Memorizing the difference between them is the single highest-yield activity for KTU exams. **(a) Batch Reactor (BR):** - **Input = Output = 0** (closed system) - Balance reduces to: $\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A \, V$ - Since $N_A = N_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ and $V$ is constant (constant density): $\frac{dX_A}{dt} = \frac{-r_A}{C_{A0}}$ **(b) Plug Flow Reactor (PFR):** - **Steady state**, **no mixing in axial direction**, **uniform radial mixing** - Balance over a differential slice $dV$: $F_{A0} \, dX_A = -r_A \, dV$ - Integrated form: $\frac{V}{F_{A0}} = \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX}{-r_A}$ - Equivalently: $\tau = \int_{C_{Af}}^{C_{A0}} \frac{dC_A}{-r_A} = \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX}{-r_A}$ **(c) Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR):** - **Steady state**, **perfect mixing** (exit composition = composition inside) - Balance reduces to: $V = \frac{F_{A0} X_A}{-r_A}$ - Or: $\tau = \frac{V}{v_0} = \frac{C_{A0} X_A}{-r_A}$ ### 2.3 Rate Laws Required for KTU Lab Simulation For the standard KTU PCCHL507 Module 2, the following kinetic forms are tested: **1. First-order irreversible:** $-r_A = k C_A$ **2. Second-order irreversible:** $-r_A = k C_A^2$ **3. Zero-order irreversible:** $-r_A = k$ **4. Arrhenius temperature dependence:** $k = k_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{-E}{RT}\right)$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of literal pipe character $\vert$ in tables to avoid Markdown breakage.** | Reactor Type | Governing Equation | ODE Form for MATLAB | Solution Variable | |---|---|---|---| | **Batch (BR)** | $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = -r_A$ | $\dot{C}_A = -k C_A^n$ | Time $t$ (s) for target $X_A$ | | **PFR** | $\dfrac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A$ | $\dfrac{dC_A}{d\tau} = -r_A$ | Volume $V$ (m³) for target $X_A$ | | **CSTR** | $V = \dfrac{F_{A0} X_A}{-r_A}$ | Algebraic (no ODE) | Volume $V$ (m³) for target $X_A$ | | **Series CSTRs ($N$ tanks)** | $V_i = \dfrac{F_{A0}(X_{A,i} - X_{A,i-1})}{-r_{A,i}}$ | Algebraic, sequential | Total $V = \sum V_i$ | | Symbol | Meaning | Typical KTU Units | |---|---|---| | $C_{A0}$ | Inlet/initial concentration | kmol/m³ | | $F_{A0}$ | Molar feed flow rate | kmol/s | | $k$ | Reaction rate constant | s⁻¹ (1st order), m³/(kmol·s) (2nd order) | | $X_A$ | Fractional conversion of $A$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | | $\tau$ | Space time | s | | $-r_A$ | Rate of disappearance of $A$ | kmol/(m³·s) | | $E$ | Activation energy | J/mol or kJ/kmol | | $T$ | Absolute temperature | K | ### 2.5 Engineering Utility - **Plant Design:** Determines the physical size (volume) of reactors before fabrication. - **Economic Optimization:** Finds the cheapest reactor configuration (e.g., one large PFR vs. two CSTRs in series). - **Safety Analysis:** Predicts runaway behavior and hot spots before commissioning. - **Scale-up:** Translates bench-scale kinetics to industrial-scale dimensions. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Analytical Solution for First-Order Reaction in a Batch Reactor **Given:** $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$, with $-r_A = k C_A$, $C_A(0) = C_{A0}$, isothermal. **Step 1:** Write the batch mole balance for species $A$. $$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A V$$ **Step 2:** Since the volume is constant (liquid-phase, isothermal, constant density assumption): $$\frac{d(V C_A)}{dt} = V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A V$$ **Step 3:** Divide both sides by $V$ and substitute $r_A = -k C_A$ (negative because $A$ is disappearing): $$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k C_A$$ **Step 4:** Separate variables to prepare for integration. Move all $C_A$ terms to the left and $t$ terms to the right: $$\frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -k \, dt$$ **Step 5:** Integrate both sides. Left side: from $C_{A0}$ to $C_A$. Right side: from $0$ to $t$: $$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} \frac{dC_A'}{C_A'} = -k \int_0^t dt'$$ **Step 6:** Evaluate the integrals. The left side yields a natural logarithm, the right side yields $-kt$: $$\ln(C_A) - \ln(C_{A0}) = -k t$$ **Step 7:** Combine the logarithms on the left using the property $\ln(a) - \ln(b) = \ln(a/b)$: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_A}{C_{A0}}\right) = -k t$$ **Step 8:** Exponentiate both sides to remove the logarithm. This is the **classical first-order decay law**: $$C_A(t) = C_{A0} \, e^{-k t}$$ **Step 9:** Convert to conversion $X_A$ using $X_A = 1 - C_A / C_{A0}$: $$X_A(t) = 1 - e^{-k t}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical check:** For $k = 0.1 \text{ s}^{-1}$ and $t = 10$ s, we get $X_A = 1 - e^{-1} = 0.632$. This means 63.2% of $A$ has reacted in 10 seconds. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: PFR Volume for First-Order Reaction **Given:** Steady-state PFR, $-r_A = k C_A$, $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$, volumetric flow rate $v_0$ constant. **Step 1:** Write the PFR design equation in terms of conversion: $$F_{A0} \frac{dX_A}{dV} = -r_A$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$: $$F_{A0} \frac{dX_A}{dV} = k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$$ **Step 3:** Separate variables. Move $dV$ to the right and group $X_A$ terms on the left: $$\frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A} = \frac{k C_{A0}}{F_{A0}} dV$$ **Step 4:** Recognize that $F_{A0}/C_{A0} = v_0$ (the volumetric flow rate). Substitute: $$\frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A} = \frac{k}{v_0} dV$$ **Step 5:** Integrate from inlet ($X_A = 0$, $V = 0$) to outlet ($X_A = X_{A,\text{out}}$, $V = V$): $$\int_0^{X_{A,\text{out}}} \frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A} = \frac{k}{v_0} \int_0^V dV$$ **Step 6:** Evaluate the left integral using the substitution $u = 1 - X_A$, $du = -dX_A$: $$-\ln(1 - X_A)\Big|_0^{X_{A,\text{out}}} = \frac{k V}{v_0}$$ **Step 7:** Evaluate the limits. At the upper limit: $-\ln(1 - X_{A,\text{out}})$. At the lower limit: $-\ln(1 - 0) = 0$: $$-\ln(1 - X_{A,\text{out}}) = \frac{k V}{v_0}$$ **Step 8:** Solve for $V$: $$V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{v_0}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - X_{A,\text{out}}}\right)$$ **Step 9:** For 90% conversion ($X_A = 0.9$): $$V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{v_0}{k} \ln(10) \approx \frac{2.303 \, v_0}{k}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3: CSTR Volume for First-Order Reaction **Given:** Steady-state CSTR, $-r_A = k C_A$, perfectly mixed. **Step 1:** Apply the steady-state CSTR balance directly: $$V = \frac{F_{A0} X_A}{-r_A}$$ **Step 2:** Express the exit rate using $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$: $$-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$$ **Step 3:** Substitute into the volume expression: $$V_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{F_{A0} X_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute $F_{A0}/C_{A0} = v_0$: $$V_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{v_0 X_A}{k(1 - X_A)}$$ **Step 5:** For 90% conversion ($X_A = 0.9$): $$V_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{v_0 (0.9)}{k (0.1)} = \frac{9 v_0}{k}$$ ### 3.4 Comparative Result (CRITICAL KTU Insight) > [!IMPORTANT] > Compare the two volumes for $X_A = 0.9$: > $$\frac{V_{\text{CSTR}}}{V_{\text{PFR}}} = \frac{9 v_0 / k}{2.303 v_0 / k} \approx 3.91$$ > > **For the same conversion, a single CSTR requires about 3.91× the volume of a PFR.** This is one of the most-tested facts in CRE and is a guaranteed KTU viva question. ### 3.5 MATLAB Implementation Logic (Analytical Matrix) | Step | Action | MATLAB Function/Command | |---|---|---| | 1 | Define parameters ($k$, $C_{A0}$, $F_{A0}$, $X_A^{\text{target}}$) | Inline assignment | | 2 | Define rate law as anonymous function | `rA = @(CA) k*CA;` | | 3 | For BR/PFR: define ODE system | `dCAdt = @(t,CA) -k*CA;` | | 4 | Integrate ODE | `[t, CA] = ode45(dCAdt, tspan, CA0);` | | 5 | Compute conversion | `XA = 1 - CA/CA0;` | | 6 | For CSTR: algebraic evaluation | `V_CSTR = FA0*XA/(-rA(CA))` | | 7 | Plot results | `plot(t, XA); xlabel('Time'); ylabel('X_A');` | | 8 | Label graph | `title('Batch Reactor Conversion vs Time');` | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 MATLAB Simulation Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Start MATLAB Simulation] --> stepB[Define Parameters: k, CA0, FA0, T, XAtarget] stepB --> stepC{Choose Reactor Type} stepC --> stepD[Batch Reactor: BR] stepC --> stepE[PFR] stepC --> stepF[CSTR] stepD --> stepG[Define ODE: dCAdt = -rA] stepE --> stepH[Define ODE: dCAdV = rA/v0] stepF --> stepI[Algebraic: V = FA0 XA / -rA] stepG --> stepJ[Call ode45 Solver] stepH --> stepJ stepI --> stepK[Direct Calculation] stepJ --> stepL[Extract CA versus t or V] stepK --> stepL stepL --> stepM[Compute XA = 1 - CA / CA0] stepM --> stepN[Plot Conversion versus Time or Volume] stepN --> stepO[Validate: Compare with Analytical Solution] stepO --> stepP[Report Final Reactor Volume or Time] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Relationship Between Ideal Reactors ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph feed[Feed Stream] FA0[F_A0 molar flow of A] CA0[C_A0 inlet concentration] end feed --> reactorChoice{Reactor Selection} reactorChoice --> batchSys[Batch Reactor
Closed, unsteady] reactorChoice --> pfrSys[Plug Flow Reactor
Tubular, steady] reactorChoice --> cstrSys[CSTR
Tank, well-mixed] batchSys --> out1[Product after time t] pfrSys --> out2[Product after volume V] cstrSys --> out3[Product after volume V] out1 --> compare[Compare Conversion and Volume] out2 --> compare out3 --> compare compare --> optimize[Select Optimal Reactor Configuration] ``` ### 4.3 Representative MATLAB Code Skeleton for the Lab Record > [!NOTE] > The following skeleton is **representative** of what KTU expects in the lab record (PCCHL507 Module 2). Students typically write this in their manual during the experiment. **For Batch Reactor (First-Order, $A \rightarrow B$):** ```matlab % Parameter block k = 0.1; % rate constant, 1/s CA0 = 1.0; % initial concentration, kmol/m^3 tspan = [0 50]; % time span, seconds % ODE definition: dCA/dt = -k*CA dCAdt = @(t, CA) -k * CA; % Solve ODE [t, CA] = ode45(dCAdt, tspan, CA0); % Convert to conversion XA = 1 - CA / CA0; % Plot figure; plot(t, XA, 'b-', 'LineWidth', 2); xlabel('Time t (s)'); ylabel('Conversion X_A'); title('Batch Reactor: Conversion vs Time (First Order)'); grid on; ``` **For CSTR (First-Order, Direct Algebraic):** ```matlab % Parameter block k = 0.1; FA0 = 0.5; % molar feed rate, kmol/s CA0 = 1.0; XA_target = 0.9; % CSTR volume V_CSTR = (FA0 * XA_target) / (k * CA0 * (1 - XA_target)); fprintf('Required CSTR volume for 90%% conversion: %.3f m^3\n', V_CSTR); ``` **For PFR (First-Order, Numerical Integration):** ```matlab % Parameter block k = 0.1; FA0 = 0.5; CA0 = 1.0; v0 = FA0 / CA0; Vspan = [0 50]; % ODE: dCA/dV = rA/v0 = -k*CA/v0 dCAdV = @(V, CA) -k * CA / v0; % Solve [V, CA] = ode45(dCAdV, Vspan, CA0); XA = 1 - CA / CA0; % Find volume for 90% conversion idx = find(XA >= 0.9, 1, 'first'); V_required = V(idx); fprintf('Required PFR volume for 90%% conversion: %.3f m^3\n', V_required); ``` ### 4.4 Typical Output Plots a Student Must Produce 1. **Batch:** $X_A$ vs $t$ (exponential rise from 0 to 1) 2. **CSTR:** $X_A$ vs $\tau$ (rises hyperbolically; reaches 0.5 at $\tau = 1/k$ for first-order) 3. **PFR:** $X_A$ vs $V$ (rises logarithmically; approaches 1 asymptotically) 4. **Comparison:** Bar chart of $V$ required for CSTR vs PFR vs BR for fixed $X_A$ ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PCCHL507] *State the design equation for a CSTR and a PFR carrying out the irreversible first-order reaction $A \rightarrow B$.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **CSTR (steady state, well-mixed):** $$V_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{F_{A0} X_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)}$$ - **PFR (steady state, plug flow):** $$V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)$$ - For $X_A = 0.9$, $V_{\text{CSTR}} \approx 3.91 \, V_{\text{PFR}}$. *(1 mark for CSTR, 1 mark for PFR, 1 mark for the comparison or units.)* --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCCHL507] *List any three advantages of simulating ideal reactors using MATLAB before performing a wet-lab experiment.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Safety:** Hazardous reactions (e.g., exothermic polymerizations) can be tested virtually without risk. 2. **Cost-effective:** Saves chemicals, reagents, glassware, and laboratory time. 3. **Speed and accuracy:** Numerical ODE solvers provide high-precision results in milliseconds, with adaptive step sizing. 4. **Easy parametric study:** Parameters like $k$, $C_{A0}$, and $T$ can be varied instantly to study sensitivity. 5. **Visualization:** Immediate graphical plots help conceptual understanding. *(Any three → 1 mark each.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT forget the negative sign** in $-r_A$ when writing design equations. Markers deduct 1 full mark if sign error occurs. > - **Always specify units** for $V$, $\tau$, and $k$. A numerical answer without units loses 0.5–1 mark. > - **For MATLAB questions**, marks are split as: (i) **algorithm/flowchart** → 4 marks, (ii) **code structure and syntax** → 5 marks, (iii) **results/plot interpretation** → 3 marks, (iv) **conclusion** → 2 marks. > - **Confusing $\tau$ with $t$**: $\tau$ is space-time (volume/flow), $t$ is real time. PFR uses $\tau$, Batch uses $t$. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** [7 Marks] *For the liquid-phase irreversible reaction $A \rightarrow B$ with rate law $-r_A = 0.25 \, C_A$ (with units kmol/m³·s), calculate the volume required for:* *(i) A single CSTR* *(ii) A single PFR* *to achieve 80% conversion of $A$. The feed conditions are $F_{A0} = 2$ kmol/min and $C_{A0} = 1.5$ kmol/m³.* **(b)** [7 Marks] *Write the MATLAB code (using `ode45`) to simulate the PFR case in part (a) and plot conversion $X_A$ versus reactor volume $V$. State the ODE being solved and explain each section of the code briefly.* --- **Solution:** **Part (a):** Given: $k = 0.25$ min⁻¹ (note unit), $X_A = 0.8$, $F_{A0} = 2$ kmol/min, $C_{A0} = 1.5$ kmol/m³. *(i) CSTR volume:* $$V_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{F_{A0} X_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)}$$ $$V_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{(2)(0.8)}{(0.25)(1.5)(0.2)} = \frac{1.6}{0.075} = 21.33 \text{ m}^3$$ *(ii) PFR volume:* $$V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - X_A}\right)$$ $$V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{2}{(0.25)(1.5)} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.2}\right) = \frac{2}{0.375} \ln(5)$$ $$V_{\text{PFR}} = 5.333 \times 1.6094 = 8.585 \text{ m}^3$$ **Comparison:** $V_{\text{CSTR}} / V_{\text{PFR}} = 21.33 / 8.585 \approx 2.485$ > [!NOTE] > The ratio for 80% conversion is smaller than for 90% (which is 3.91) — students must understand this trend. **Part (b):** MATLAB code skeleton: ```matlab % Parameters k = 0.25; % rate constant, 1/min CA0 = 1.5; % inlet concentration, kmol/m^3 FA0 = 2; % molar feed rate, kmol/min v0 = FA0 / CA0; % volumetric flow rate, m^3/min Vspan = [0 30]; % volume range, m^3 % ODE: dCA/dV = -rA/v0 = -(k*CA)/v0 dCAdV = @(V, CA) -(k * CA) / v0; % Solve using ode45 [V, CA] = ode45(dCAdV, Vspan, CA0); % Conversion XA = 1 - CA / CA0; % Plot figure; plot(V, XA, 'r-', 'LineWidth', 2); xlabel('Reactor Volume V (m^3)'); ylabel('Conversion X_A'); title('PFR: Conversion vs Volume (First-Order A to B)'); grid on; ``` **Explanation (1 mark per point):** - **Parameters block:** Declares all constants in SI-consistent units. - **ODE definition:** Translates the PFR design equation $dC_A/dV = r_A/v_0$ into MATLAB syntax using an anonymous function handle `@(V, CA)`. - **ode45 call:** Uses the Dormand-Prince RK45 method for non-stiff ODEs with adaptive step sizing. - **Conversion:** $X_A = 1 - C_A/C_{A0}$ converts concentration to fractional conversion. - **Plot:** Visualizes how conversion approaches 1.0 as volume increases. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Alternative] **(a)** [7 Marks] *Derive the integrated form of the design equation for an isothermal batch reactor in which the first-order irreversible reaction $A \rightarrow B$ takes place. Hence compute the time required for 75% conversion if $k = 0.05$ min⁻¹.* **(b)** [7 Marks] *Explain how MATLAB's `ode45` solver is used to simulate a series CSTR system (two CSTRs in series) for the reaction in part (a). Show the system of algebraic equations that must be solved sequentially and the corresponding MATLAB logic (pseudo-code or actual code is acceptable).* --- **Solution:** **Part (a): Derivation:** Start from the batch mole balance (Step 1): $\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A V$ For constant volume: $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = -k C_A$ (Step 2) Separate variables: $\dfrac{dC_A}{C_A} = -k \, dt$ (Step 3) Integrate from $C_{A0}$ to $C_A$ and $0$ to $t$: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_A}{C_{A0}}\right) = -k t$$ $$C_A(t) = C_{A0} e^{-k t}$$ Convert to conversion: $X_A(t) = 1 - e^{-k t}$ For $X_A = 0.75$ and $k = 0.05$ min⁻¹: $$0.75 = 1 - e^{-0.05 t}$$ $$e^{-0.05 t} = 0.25$$ $$-0.05 t = \ln(0.25) = -1.3863$$ $$t = 27.73 \text{ min}$$ **Part (b): Two CSTRs in series:** For CSTR 1: $$V_1 = \frac{F_{A0} X_{A1}}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_{A1})}$$ For CSTR 2 (using exit of CSTR 1 as feed): $$V_2 = \frac{F_{A0}(X_{A2} - X_{A1})}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_{A2})}$$ Total volume: $V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2$ **MATLAB logic:** ```matlab k = 0.05; CA0 = 1.0; FA0 = 1.0; XA_target = 0.75; % Guess XA1 (conversion leaving CSTR 1) XA1_guess = 0.4; % Solve for XA1 such that total conversion is XA_target % Using fsolve (system of algebraic equations) fun = @(XA1) deal([], ... (FA0*XA1/(k*CA0*(1-XA1))) + ... (FA0*(XA_target - XA1)/(k*CA0*(1-XA_target))) - V_total); ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > For two CSTRs in series with equal volumes, the optimal split of $X_{A1}$ and $X_{A2}$ minimizes total $V$. This is a classic KTU viva question. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin this in your lab record:** - ✅ The **three ideal reactors** are **Batch (BR), PFR, and CSTR** — each has a unique design equation. - ✅ For **Batch**: time-based ODE; $t = -\ln(1 - X_A)/k$ for first-order. - ✅ For **PFR**: volume-based ODE; $V = (F_{A0}/kC_{A0})\ln[1/(1 - X_A)]$ for first-order. - ✅ For **CSTR**: algebraic; $V = F_{A0}X_A/[kC_{A0}(1 - X_A)]$ for first-order. - ✅ **PFR is always smaller than CSTR** for the same conversion (positive-order kinetics). - ✅ **Two CSTRs in series < single CSTR** for the same total volume (for positive-order reactions). - ✅ **MATLAB function `ode45`** is the workhorse for non-stiff ODEs; use `ode15s` for stiff systems (e.g., Robertson reaction). - ✅ Always use **anonymous function handles** `@(t, CA) ...` for clean ODE syntax. - ✅ The **general mole balance** $F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = dN_A/dt$ is the master equation — every design equation is derived from it. - ✅ For **multiple reactions** (series $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$): write one ODE per species, solve as a system. - ✅ **Always convert units consistently** — common KTU pitfall: $k$ in s⁻¹ vs min⁻¹ vs hr⁻¹. - ✅ **Arrhenius equation** $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$ is needed if temperature varies (non-isothermal).
